entry.title: Cutover control for SAP Cloud Integration: CSV driven bulk shutdown and wave based start of iFlows
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

I am working on a large ECC to S/4HANA migration where the SAP Cloud Integration tenant stays the same .Existing iFlows are repointed to S/4HANA rather than rebuilt. The cutover therefore requires interfaces talking to ECC to be stopped before data migration, repointed, and brought back up in a controlled sequence. Crucially, only the iFlows touching the migrating SAP system need to stop; the same tenant runs third party and non-SAP interfaces that must keep running. So "undeploy everything" is not an option . What is needed is selective undeploy and deploy of a defined list and this is exactly what this tool does.

On SAP PI/PO this was a solved problem: PIMON let you filter, multi-select and stop/start channels centrally, with status visible in the same place. CPI has no equivalent . The web UI handles one artifact at a time, which in a cutover window is slow and error prone. The model also differs: PI stopped the channel while the scenario stayed in place; in CPI the lever is undeploying the artifact from the runtime — design untouched, but stopping means removing the deployed artifact.

REQUIREMENT


The case for a tool comes down to two numbers — time and error rate. At real cutover scale, say 250 iFlows in scope:

  1. Reduce time. Manually, each undeploy is filter, select, click Undeploy, confirm, verify — 30 to 45 seconds per iFlow on a responsive tenant. That is close to 3 hours of pure clicking for 250 iFlows, and with scope cross-checking, evidence capture and interruptions, realistically 4 to 6 hours, usually split across several people — which adds its own coordination risk of items falling between the splits. The tool executes the same 250 undeploys via the API in under 10 minutes.

  2. Reduce error rate. Human accuracy on a repetitive 250-item selection task over hours is not 100%. Even a 1–2% slip rate means 2 to 5 wrong outcomes: an in-scope iFlow missed and still pumping messages into ECC during data migration, or an out-of-scope one undeployed — taking down a live interface that had nothing to do with the cutover. Worst of all, manual misses are silent. With the tool, the scope is a reviewed CSV and the follow-up -status run is a complete, deterministic audit of all 250 — not a memory of what got clicked.

    This blog describes a command line tool I built to solve this .The setup is:

    CSV list of iFlows --> Java CLI tool --> CPI OData API (v1) --> CPI runtime

    During cutover we needed:

    1. A reviewed and signed off list of in-scope iFlows

    2. A status check before the shutdown window (everything STARTED)

    3. A bulk undeploy of exactly that list

    4. A status check after (everything gone from runtime

    5. Console output that can be pasted into the cutover log as evidence

Only the interfaces in scope should be touched. Other integrations on the tenant keep running.


THE TOOL

The tool is a single jar Java 21 application taking three arguments:

java -jar cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar <config-file> <iflows-csv> <-status|-undeploy|-deploy>

The iFlow list is a two column CSV — package and iFlow name:

Package,iFlow Name
PACKAGEMONTHLYEXTRACTINTERFACES,MONTHLY_TAX_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_BILLING_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_MASTER_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_FI_DATA


Note the Package column contains technical package Ids.

When the cutover task says "stop in-scope interfaces", the definition of in-scope is this file.

 

GOTCHA : Package name and Package Id are not the same thing


This one cost me a debugging session. I first populated the CSV from the Manage Integration Content monitor, where every deployed iFlow shows a Package field:

The first run failed for every iFlow with "iFlow not found in package" — even though the monitor showed all of them as Started:




The reason: the Package field in the monitor is the package display name, but the OData API keys on the technical package Id — and the two differ. The Id is auto-generated from the name with special characters (including underscores) stripped, so a package named PACKAGE_OTC_OBJECTS has the Id PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS:



The same applies to artifacts — an iFlow has a display name and a technical Id, and the API wants the Id. In my case the two matched (the monitor shows both, under Name and ID), but do not assume they do.

Where to find the technical Ids:

  • Open the package in Design — the Package ID is on the Overview tab and in the browser URL (.../shell/design/contentpackage/<Id>)

  • Or query the API directly:

curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \

"https://<tmn-host>/api/v1/IntegrationPackages?$select=Id,Name&$format=json"

With the technical Ids in the CSV, the same run succeeds:

One more useful behaviour: for -status the package column can be left empty. The runtime API only needs the iFlow Id, so the tool skips package resolution and queries IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts directly:

Rule of thumb: the CSV takes technical Ids, not the display names in the monitor. For status-only runs the package column is optional; for deploy it must be correct, because the design-time lookup depends on it.

CHECKING STATUS

java -jar target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar config.properties iflows.csv -status

This prints the runtime status of each iFlow in the CSV:

+---------+---------------------------------+------------------+
| STATUS | PACKAGE | IFLOW |
+---------+---------------------------------+------------------+
| STARTED | PACKAGEMONTHLYEXTRACTINTERFACES | MONTHLY_TAX_DATA |
| STARTED | PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS | OTC_BILLING_DATA |
| STARTED | PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS | OTC_MASTER_DATA |
| STARTED | PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS | OTC_FI_DATA |
+---------+---------------------------------+------------------+
Summary: 4 succeeded, 0 failed (4 total)

Status is run at two points in the cutover: before the shutdown window to confirm the baseline (everything STARTED), and after the undeploy to confirm the interfaces are down. The two captures become the before/after evidence for the runbook verification task. Beyond cutover, the same command doubles as a quick health check across a defined interface list — after a tenant update or during hypercare. A useful side effect: it surfaces artifacts already in ERROR state, which you want to find before the cutover window, not during it.

UNDEPLOYING

java -jar target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar config.properties iflows.csv -undeploy

This walks the same CSV and undeploys each artifact via the OData v1 API (IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts). The summary line shows immediately if anything failed.
An important detail: undeploy is asynchronous in CPI. The OData call only submits the request — the API accepts it and returns, while removal from the runtime node happens in the background. A successful call means "the stop was requested", not "the interface is down". If an iFlow is mid-message, the runtime winds that down first. This is visible in Manage Integration Content right after the bulk undeploy — all four iFlows sit in Stopping state.

This is exactly why status matters in the shutdown sequence. After -undeploy, run -status against the same CSV; if some artifacts still show up, wait and re-run until the list is clear. The evidence that satisfies the verification task is the status output showing the interfaces are down — not the undeploy summary showing requests were submitted. The undeploy is the trigger; the status check is the confirmation.

Configuration:

Connectivity uses a properties file for the credentials

cpi.base.url=https://your-tenant.it-cpi018.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
cpi.auth.type=oauth2
cpi.oauth.token.url=https://your-tenant.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
cpi.oauth.client.id=your-client-id
cpi.oauth.client.secret=your-client-secret


One config file per tenant: the same CSV is exercised against test during cutover rehearsal, then replayed against production by changing a single argument.

BTP service instance, plan and roles

The service key comes from a Process Integration Runtime instance with the api plan (not *integration-flow*, which is only for calling deployed iFlows). Create the instance in the BTP cockpit under Services > Service Marketplace, select the roles at creation, and create a service key with grant type client_credentials. The clientid, clientsecret, tokenurl and url map directly to config.properties above.

Three roles are needed, mapped to what each mode does:

Role

Used for

MonitoringDataRead

-status — reading runtime artifact status via IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts

WorkspacePackagesRead

Resolving iFlows inside a package at design time (IntegrationPackages / IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts)

WorkspaceArtifactsDeploy

-deploy and -undeploy — deploying and undeploying artifacts

These are set as parameters at instance creation. The exact configuration the tool has been verified against:

The token-validity of 43200 seconds gives a 12 hour token — longer than any cutover window, so the tool requests one token per run and never refreshes mid-sequence. Role names per API operation are listed in the SAP Help Portal under "Tasks and Permissions" for the Cloud Integration OData API.

A recommendation from experience: create a dedicated instance and key with only these roles, rather than reusing a broad integration developer key or an AuthGroup role — least privilege applies to cutover tooling too. For status-only runs (e.g. a hypercare health check), MonitoringDataRead and WorkspacePackagesRead are enough. And keep the filled-in config.properties out of Git — the repo commits templates only.

Controlled start: using deploy mode in waves

Deployment

The tool also supports a deploy mode:

java -jar target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar config.properties iflows-wave1.csv -deploy

However, shutdown and startup are not used the same way. In this migration the deploy step matters even more: between shutdown and startup the iFlows are repointed from ECC to S/4HANA — connection details, externalized parameters and endpoints updated — so each wave brings up the reconfigured interface, validated wave by wave against the new environment.

Shutdown is a blanket action — everything in scope must be down before migration starts, so one bulk undeploy of the full CSV is exactly right. Startup is a controlled start — interfaces return in a deliberate sequence, typically master data flows first and then transactional, gated by smoke tests and sign-offs between waves. Starting everything in one shot would flood a freshly migrated system with queued messages before anyone has validated the first interface.
At the same time, a wave is rarely a single interface — a master data wave might hold a dozen iFlows that come up together, and deploying those one by one through the UI has the same problem as the shutdown, just smaller. So the same CSV driven approach applies, with one CSV per wave:

Running: deploy with a wave CSV brings up all its iFlows in one command. Deploy is asynchronous like undeploy — the call submits, the runtime takes time to start — so the pattern repeats: -deploy the wave, then -status against the same CSV until everything shows STARTED, then run the smoke tests. Only after sign-off does the next wave go up.

The exercise on a trial 

I ran the full sequence on my tenant with the four iFlows split into two waves: all four stopped in one bulk undeploy (the Stopping screenshot above), then wave 1 (the three OTC iFlows) and wave 2 (MONTHLY_TAX_DATA) started separately.

Deploying wave 1 brings up all three OTC iFlows in one command — the DEPLOYING result confirms the requests were accepted

The asynchronous start is visible in the UI moments later — OTC_BILLING_DATA is already Started while the other two are still Starting:

Once the runtime finishes, -status against wave1.csv confirms all three STARTED — this is the check before wave 1 smoke tests:

 

Only then does wave 2 go up the same way:

Sequencing between waves stays a runbook decision. The tool automates everything within a wave — one command instead of a dozen clicks, with the status check confirming it.

GitHub Dev Containers

For this tool, and for development in general

The part I want to highlight is the environment. The classic failure mode of a utility like this: it works on the author's laptop, but the person executing the cutover task has JDK 17 instead of 21, or no Maven, or a proxy issue — and a five minute task becomes an hour of environment debugging inside the cutover window.

The repo ships with a .devcontainer definition: a small JSON file (plus optionally a Dockerfile) committed to the repo, describing the complete development environment — runtime versions, build tools, CLI utilities, VS Code extensions and settings. Open the repo and VS Code builds a container from it; your editor works inside that container.

How this differs from a traditional IDE setup

With a traditional IDE setup (Eclipse, IntelliJ, or plain VS Code on the host), the environment lives on the machine and only the project lives in the repo. JDK version, Maven settings, proxy config and plugins are per-machine state set up by hand, usually from a stale wiki page. Two people on the same project routinely have different environments, and nobody can prove which is correct.

With a dev container, the environment lives in the repo, next to the code:

  • The environment is versioned. When the tool moves to a newer Java version, the dev container moves in the same commit. Checking out an old tag gives you the old environment too — no traditional IDE setup offers that.

  • Onboarding is one click. Clone, "Reopen in Container", done. No install guide, no "which JDK" thread. Extensions and settings come along, so even the editor is consistent.

  • The host stays clean. Projects with conflicting toolchains (Java 17 here, Java 21 there, different Node versions for CAP projects) coexist without nvm/sdkman juggling — each project brings its own container.

  • It runs in the browser. GitHub Codespaces runs the same definition on a cloud VM with zero local install — genuinely useful when the person on cutover duty has a locked down laptop where installing a JDK needs a service ticket.

The trade-offs are small: Docker Desktop must be installed and licensed on the host (Codespaces avoids even this), the first build takes a few minutes, and Windows file I/O is slower unless the repo lives in WSL2. For most SAP side-car development these costs are negligible against the consistency gained.

This isn't unique to this tool. For a great walkthrough of the same pattern applied to SAP CAP development - showing how one dev container definition runs identically across local VS Code, GitHub Codespaces, and Business Application Studio - see DJ Adams' Developing CAP in containers – three ways.

Dev containers as a development practice, not just for this tool

I now treat the dev container as part of the deliverable for anything I build outside the ABAP stack, and it fits BTP development well — CAP (Node.js and Java), the supporting tooling that accumulates around a CPI workstream, and UI5/Fiori all benefit from pinned runtime and tooling versions, removing the "works for me" drift between developers entirely.

ABAP itself stays in the ABAP stack — but nearly everything around it in a modern SAP landscape is Java or Node, and all of it can be containerised this way.

For SAP integration teams this matters more than in mainstream web development. The team mixes ABAP developers, middleware specialists and functional consultants — you cannot assume a curated local Java setup. The dev container turns "build and run this Java tool" into a task anyone can execute the same way.

The cutover rehearsal workflow

Open the repo in Codespaces (or reopen locally in the dev container)

mvn clean package --> target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar
Copy the config and CSV templates; fill in tenant details and the signed off interface list

Run -status, capture, run -undeploy, run -status again, capture

Paste both tables into the cutover log

Conclusion

  • Cutover integration shutdown becomes deterministic with a CSV driven bulk undeploy instead of clicking through the CPI UI.
  • Package name and package Id are different — the monitor shows display names, the OData API wants technical Ids (auto-generated with special characters stripped). Populate the CSV with Ids.
  • The CSV doubles as the signed off shutdown scope; the before/after status tables double as cutover evidence.
    Undeploy is asynchronous in CPI — the call submits the stop request; the status check confirms the interfaces are down. Undeploy is the trigger, status is the evidence.
  • Ramp-up uses the same tool in deploy mode with wave specific CSVs — all iFlows in a wave deploy together in one command, while sequencing between waves (master data first, then transactional) stays a runbook decision gated by smoke tests and sign-offs.
  • A GitHub Dev Container means anyone can build and run the tool identically, including in the browser via Codespaces — and the same practice pays off for day to day CAP, CPI tooling and UI5 development, where the environment is versioned with the code instead of living per-machine. 

The code is on GitHub: https://github.com/viksingh/sap-cpi-iflow-controller

entry.title: Unlock value with SAP Integration Suite: Quarterly roadmap insights
entry.content:
New quarter. New innovations. New opportunities.

SAP Integration Suite continues to evolve, and every update creates new ways to help customers integrate systems faster, streamline business processes, and accelerate transformation.

Want the highlights without spending hours digging through roadmap updates?

Join our webinar, "Unlock value with SAP Integration Suite: Quarterly roadmap insights". We'll cover the biggest innovations from the past quarter, preview what's ahead, and dive into selected capabilities that every SAP Partner should know.

Whether you work in sales, presales, consulting, or delivery, these insights can help you turn roadmap updates into customer value and stronger engagements.

Don't miss your quarterly roadmap update.

Register now and stay ahead of what's next. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013385.html

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 2: Testing with Postman & CPI iFlow Integration
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 2: Testing with Postman & CPI iFlow Integration

Part 2 of a 4-part beginner series on SAP Event Mesh.

A quick recap

In Part 1, we covered the "why" and the "what" — Event-Driven Architecture, the publish-subscribe model, and the three building blocks of SAP Event Mesh: topics, queues, and namespaces. We then provisioned an Event Mesh instance on SAP BTP (region AP11), created queues for each consumer system, subscribed those queues to topics, and generated a service key holding our connection credentials.

👉If you haven't read it yet, start with Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture, Topics & Queues — this post builds directly on that setup. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-1-event-driven-architecture-topics-queues/bc-p/14428494#M179525 

So we have a working Event Mesh instance. But how do we actually send a message into it, and how do we get SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) to publish and consume events? That's what we'll do here.

We'll work in two stages:

  1. Validate the setup with Postman — prove that authentication works and that we can publish a message before we touch CPI.
  2. Build the CPI iFlows — one to publish events over HTTP, and one to consume them over AMQP.

Here's the picture we're building toward:

 

 

Let's get started.

3. Testing with Postman

Why test with Postman first? Because if something goes wrong later in CPI, we want to know the Event Mesh side is already working. Postman lets us isolate the problem. It's a habit worth building.

Step 1 — Configure OAuth2 authentication

Open Postman, create a new request, and go to the Authorization tab. We'll set it up like this:

Field Value
Auth TypeOAuth 2.0
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Access Token URLhttps://integrationsuite-<your-subdomain>.authentication.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
Client ID<uaa.clientid from your service key>
Client Secret<uaa.clientsecret from your service key>
Scope(leave empty)
Client AuthenticationSend client credentials in body

 

 

Now here's the single most common mistake people make, and it's worth pausing on:

⚠️Watch out: The Event Mesh Client ID contains special characters such as | and !. If we send the credentials as a Basic Auth header, those characters get corrupted during encoding, and we get a confusing invalid_client: Bad credentials error — even though our credentials are perfectly correct. The fix is to choose "Send client credentials in body." Always.

Click Get New Access Token. If everything is correct, Postman returns a token. We're authenticated. 🎉

Step 2 — Publish a message to a topic

Now let's send our first event. We use the messaging REST endpoint from the service key:

 
 
POST https://integrationsuite{}.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com     /messagingrest/v1/topics/{encoded-topic-name}/messages

Notice {encoded-topic-name}. This is where beginners lose an hour, so let's be careful.

The URL-encoding rule:

⚠️Every / in the topic name must be encoded as %2F — not %2. The trailing F is required. Leave it off and you'll get a 404 or, worse, silent wrong-topic routing.

So our topic name transforms like this:

  Value
Topic name (plain)S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify/CustomerMaster
Topic name (URL-encoded)S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster

Which gives us the full URL:

 
 
https://integrationsuite {}-pubsub.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/messagingrest/v1/topics/S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster/messages

Next, set the headers:

Header Value
Content-Typeapplication/json
x-qos0

A quick note on x-qos (quality of service): 0 means at most once (fire and forget — fast, but no delivery guarantee), and 1 means at least once (guaranteed delivery). For our test, 0 is fine.

Finally, the request body — a simple CloudEvents-style payload:

 
 
json
{
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "type": "S4HC.Testing.Masterdata.Shopify.CustomerMaster",
  "source": "/S4HC/Testing/Masterdata",
  "data": {
    "CustomerID": "C001",
    "Name": "Test Customer"
  }
}

 

Hit Send. And now for the part that surprises everyone:

✅A successful publish returns HTTP 204 No Content. There's no response body, and that's correct. It does not mean something went wrong — it means the message was accepted and is now sitting in the queue, waiting for a consumer.

Step 3 — Verify the message arrived

Let's confirm. Go to the Event Mesh UI → Queues, click the queue you subscribed (for example S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify), and check the message count — it should have gone up by one.

 

That's it — we've published an event to Event Mesh and confirmed it landed in the right queue, all without touching CPI yet. Now we know the foundation is solid.

4. CPI iFlow Configuration

Time to bring CPI into the picture. We'll set up the shared credential once, then build the publish and consume sides.

4.1 Create the OAuth2 credential in Security Material

Both of our iFlows will authenticate the same way, so we create the credential once and reuse it.

In CPI, go to Monitor → Security Material → Add, and select OAuth2 Client Credentials:

Field Value
Credential NameEventMeshPOC (remember this name — we'll reference it in the adapters)
Grant TypeClient Credentials
Token Service URLhttps://integration-<your-subdomain>.authentication.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
Client ID<uaa.clientid from your service key>
Client Secret<uaa.clientsecret from your service key>
Send BodyYes

 

⚠️Same gotcha as Postman: make sure Send Body is set to Yes. The special characters in the Client ID will break Basic Auth.

4.2 Publishing messages — use the HTTP adapter

Here's an important design decision, and it catches a lot of people:

⚠️Known CPI behaviour: The AMQP adapter automatically lowercases topic and queue names. So CustomerMaster silently becomes customermaster, the topic doesn't match, and we get amqp:not-allowed (error 220041). To publish, we sidestep this entirely by using the HTTP Receiver adapter instead, which preserves the exact case.

In your publisher iFlow, add an HTTP Receiver adapter:

Connection tab

Field Value
URLhttps://enterprise-messaging-pubsub.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/messagingrest/v1/topics/S4HC%2FTesting%2FMasterdata%2FShopify%2FCustomerMaster/messages
MethodPOST
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Credential NameEventMeshPOC
Content Typeapplication/json

Then, just before the HTTP adapter, add a Content Modifier step to set the required headers:

Header Name Value
Content-Typeapplication/json
x-qos0

 

That's the publish side done. When this iFlow runs, it POSTs the payload to the topic exactly the way our Postman test did — and we already know that works.

4.3 Consuming messages — use the AMQP adapter

When CPI is the consumer (the subscriber listening for events), we use the AMQP Sender adapter. And here's the key: we consume from a Queue, not a Topic — which conveniently avoids the lowercase issue we hit on the publish side.

AMQP Connection tab

Field Value
Hostenterprise-messaging-messaging-gateway.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com
Port443
Path/protocols/amqp10ws
Proxy TypeInternet
Connect with TLSChecked
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Credential NameEventMeshPOC

⚠️The Host field should contain only the hostname — no wss:// prefix. The protocol path goes in the Path field separately (/protocols/amqp10ws). Mixing them up gives errors like wss: Name or service not known or a failed WebSocket handshake.

AMQP Processing tab

Field Value
Destination TypeQueue
Queue NameS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify
DeliveryPersistent

 

✅Tip: Using Queue as the destination type (rather than Topic) on the consume side keeps the exact name intact. Reading from a queue does not have the lowercase problem that publishing to a topic does.

Putting it together

Let's step back and look at what we've built:

  • We validated authentication and publishing with Postman, so we knew the Event Mesh side worked before writing a single iFlow.
  • On the publish side, our CPI iFlow uses the HTTP Receiver adapter to POST events to a topic — preserving case and avoiding the AMQP lowercase trap.
  • On the consume side, a second iFlow uses the AMQP Sender adapter pointed at a Queue to pull events as they arrive.
  • Both adapters share a single OAuth2 Client-Credentials credential, sent in the body.

The two recurring themes — case sensitivity and credentials in the body — are exactly the things that cause the most head-scratching for beginners, so keep them front of mind.

What's next

We now have a complete round trip: events flowing from CPI into Event Mesh and back out again. But the first time you wire this up, something will break — that's normal, and it's where real learning happens.

In Part 3 — Troubleshooting & Quick Reference, we'll go through the exact errors you're most likely to hit (invalid_client, amqp:not-allowed, the %2F trap, WebSocket handshake failures), what causes each one, and how to fix it — plus a one-page cheat sheet of every URL and value you need.

If this helped, a like or a comment is always appreciated — and feel free to share what you're integrating with Event Mesh. See you in Part 3!

 

Primary Tag: SAP Integration Suite Tags: SAP Event Mesh, Cloud Integration, SAP CPI, AMQP, OAuth2, Postman, SAP BTP, Event-Driven Architecture

entry.title: Lernen verbindet – Entdecke unsere 🇩🇪 deutschsprachigen 🇩🇪 Live Sessions in Q3
entry.content:
Q3 ist in vollem Gange. Welche Themen möchtest du dieses Quartal noch entdecken?

Vielleicht gibt es ein Thema, das schon länger auf deiner Lernliste steht. Vielleicht möchtest du dein Wissen vertiefen oder einfach neue Impulse mitnehmen.

Unsere deutschsprachigen SAP BTP Live Sessions bieten die Gelegenheit dazu. Von Grundlagen über Integration und Entwicklung bis hin zu Architektur- und Automatisierungsthemen wartet ein vielfältiges Lernangebot auf dich.

Vielleicht ist genau die Session, die dich weiterbringt, bereits Teil des aktuellen Programms:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-platform-learning-group-blog-posts/new-sap-btp-live-sessions-auf-deutsch/ba-p/14415241

Also welches Thema möchtest du in Q3 noch entdecken?

Wir sehen uns in den Live Sessions!

entry.title: Building MCP Servers with SAP Integration Suite: What to Design, Not Just How to Connect
entry.content:
The MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite lets you expose any API as an MCP tool directly from API Management — giving AI agents governed, discoverable access to your systems, whether SAP or non-SAP. Getting the connection working is the easy part. The harder question is: what should your MCP tools actually look like?

What MCP adds on top of your APIs

Enterprise landscapes are rarely uniform. A single business process might touch an OData service in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, a REST API from Salesforce or ServiceNow, a SOAP service in a legacy SAP ECC system, and an RFC function module — each with different conventions and varying levels of documentation. For APIs that lack a proper spec, Integration Suite includes AI-assisted OpenAPI specification generation.

MCP provides a single, consistent interface on top of all of these:

  • Business intent — the tool description tells the agent what the operation means and when to use it
  • Multi-API orchestration — one tool can encapsulate calls across OData, REST, SOAP, and RFC — regardless of vendor
  • Governance — authentication, rate limiting, and audit logging applied consistently to every agent call
  • Broad system coverage — any system reachable via REST, OData, SOAP, RFC, or HTTP

Two ways to design your MCP tools — and why it matters

The endpoint-shaped approach (and its problems)

The natural first step is to expose existing endpoints as MCP tools one-to-one. This works for a demo, but creates problems in practice.

An agent is asked: "Create a new customer for Acme Corp, shipping address in Frankfurt, assigned to sales org DE01."

With endpoint-shaped tools:

Tool: get_business_partner_roles()
Tool: get_company_codes()
Tool: get_sales_organizations()
Tool: create_business_partner(name="Acme Corp", category="2")
Tool: create_business_partner_address(bp_id="...", city="Frankfurt", ...)
Tool: assign_business_partner_role(bp_id="...", role="FLCU01")
Tool: assign_customer_company(bp_id="...", company_code="DE01")
Tool: assign_customer_sales_area(bp_id="...", sales_org="DE01", ...)

Eight tool calls for one user request — each costing tokens and adding latency. The agent must track IDs across calls, reason about partial failures, and reconstruct SAP business logic on every run. If a business rule changes, every prompt that references it needs updating.

The intent-shaped approach

Tool: create_customer(
  name: "Acme Corp",
  city: "Frankfurt",
  country: "DE",
  sales_org: "DE01"
)

One call. The MCP server handles the sequence, enforces business rules, and manages error handling. The agent stays focused on the conversation.

What makes a good intent-shaped tool

  • Named after a business operation, not an API resource (approve_purchase_requisition, not patch_pr_item)
  • Business-meaningful parameters — accept sales_org: "DE01", not a raw ID the agent has to look up
  • Hides the sequence — the agent doesn't need to know that address creation must precede role assignment
  • Encodes business rules — the tool implementation handles logic the agent shouldn't need to reason about: which roles are mandatory, whether the requester has approval authority, what "overdue" means in your context
  • Returns a useful response — a confirmation the agent can relay, not a raw JSON dump

Tool descriptions matter

The tool description is what the agent reads to decide whether and how to use a tool.

Useful:

approve_purchase_requisition — Approves a purchase requisition in SAP S/4HANA. Use this when a user wants to approve a pending PR. Checks approval authority, updates the requisition status, adds the approval note, and advances the workflow to the purchasing team.

Not useful:

PATCH endpoint for purchase requisition status update.

How to build this with SAP Integration Suite

Joule / 3rd Party AI Agent
              ↓  MCP protocol
    MCP Gateway (SAP Integration Suite)
       ↓                  ↓                    ↓
  API Artifact       HTTP Endpoint       RFC Destination
  (OData/REST/SOAP)  (any HTTP service)  (S/4HANA / ECC RFCs)
       ↓
  optional: Cloud Integration iFlow
  (multi-system orchestration)
       ↓
  Any Backend (SAP, Salesforce, ServiceNow, legacy systems, ...)

Three creation paths are supported — choose based on your backend:

SourceWhen to use
Managed API artifact (OData / REST / SOAP)Any API already managed in API Management; add mediation steps inside the artifact if needed
HTTP endpoint + OpenAPI specAny external HTTP service
RFC destinationDirect access to SAP S/4HANA or ECC function modules

For multi-system orchestration, build a Cloud Integration iFlow, expose it as a managed API artifact, and create the MCP server from that.

Once the source is configured: select the operations to expose as tools, write a tool description for each, apply governance policies (authentication, rate limiting, audit logging), and publish to Developer Hub.

For step-by-step setup, see Design APIs and MCP Servers in the SAP Help Portal.

Before you publish: a quick checklist

  • Are your tools intent-shaped? If an agent needs to call three or more tools in sequence for a single user request, consider consolidating them.
  • Are business rules in the server, not the prompt? Sequencing logic and mandatory field rules belong in the MCP server, not in system prompts.
  • Do your tool descriptions explain intent? Write for an agent that understands business language, not for a developer reading API docs.
  • Is every call governed? Authentication, rate limiting, and audit logging should be applied at the API Management layer.
  • Do errors help the agent respond? Return "No business partner found with ID 1234567. Search by name using find_business_partner." — not { error: "PARTNER_NOT_FOUND", code: 404 }.
  • Are write operations clearly named? Tools that post documents, send emails, or trigger workflows should be named and described so the agent and user understand the consequences.

Summary

SAP Integration Suite gives you the complete stack: Cloud Integration for business logic and orchestration, API Management for governance, and the MCP Gateway to surface everything as MCP tools for any compatible AI client.

Build tools around what users want to accomplish — create_customer, approve_purchase_requisition, find_overdue_invoices — not around what endpoints exist. One well-designed tool that handles a complete business operation is more reliable, faster, and cheaper than a chain of granular API calls.

entry.title: Modernizing SAP Journal Entry Integration: From SOAP to REST with API Management
entry.content:
Introduction

Standard SAP Journal Entry APIs are typically exposed as SOAP services. While they work well within SAP landscapes, they often introduce challenges when integrating with external systems, especially those expecting REST-based interfaces.

In a recent project, this approach was used to expose Journal Entry functionality to an external non-SAP application, where the SOAP interface introduced integration complexity and increased development effort.

Understanding the Standard Journal Entry API

SAP provides standard SOAP-based APIs for Journal Entry processing (such as JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In), enabling the creation of accounting documents in S/4HANA.

For reference, SAP official API documentation can be found here: Journal Entry - Post (Synchronous)

Example of SOAP payload structure

Why This Becomes a Challenge

In many integration scenarios, especially when exposing services to external systems or partners, SOAP-based interfaces introduce a few challenges:

  • Complex XML payload structure
  • Larger message sizes
  • Harder consumption for non-SAP systems
  • Higher processing and transformation effort

These aspects can directly impact both development effort and operational costs, particularly in high-volume scenarios.

A REST-Oriented Integration Approach

To address these challenges, an integration layer was introduced using SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration), enabling transformation from SOAP to REST.

High-level architecture: REST exposure over standard SAP SOAP APIs using Integration Suite

Example Request Transformation

The REST payload was designed using a simplified and standardized naming convention aligned with common SAP terminology:

{
  "journalEntry": {
    "documentReferenceID": "REF_001",
    "companyCode": "1000",
    "documentDate": "2026-01-15",
    "postingDate": "2026-01-15",
    "items": [
      {
        "glAccount": "400000",
        "amount": 150.00,
        "itemText": "Expense Posting 01",
        "assignmentReference": "REF",
        "accountAssignment": {
          "internalOrder": "900123",
          "costCenter": "CC1000"
        }
      },
      {
        "glAccount": "200000",
        "amount": -150.00,
        "itemText": "Expense Posting 02",
        "assignmentReference": "REF",
        "accountAssignment": {
          "profitCenter": "PC1000"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

The payload structure was simplified using standardized and generalized field naming to focus on readability and integration usability, without exposing real business data.

Below is an example of how the simplified REST payload is transformed into the SOAP structure required by the standard SAP API, highlighting the mapping between a consumer-friendly model and the SAP-specific format:

<soap:Envelope xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">
  <soap:Body>
    <ns0:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest xmlns:ns0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN">      
      <MessageHeader>
        <CreationDateTime>2026-01-15T10:00:00.000Z</CreationDateTime>
      </MessageHeader>
      <JournalEntryCreateRequest>
        <MessageHeader>
          <CreationDateTime>2026-01-15T10:00:00.000Z</CreationDateTime>
        </MessageHeader>
        <JournalEntry>
          <OriginalReferenceDocumentType>BKPFF</OriginalReferenceDocumentType>
          <BusinessTransactionType>RFBU</BusinessTransactionType>
          <AccountingDocumentType>SA</AccountingDocumentType>
          <DocumentReferenceID>REF_001</DocumentReferenceID>
          <CreatedByUser>API_USER</CreatedByUser>
          <CompanyCode>1000</CompanyCode>
          <DocumentDate>2026-01-15</DocumentDate>
          <PostingDate>2026-01-15</PostingDate>
          <Item>
            <GLAccount>400000</GLAccount>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="BRL">150.00</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DocumentItemText>Expense Posting 01</DocumentItemText>
            <AssignmentReference>REF</AssignmentReference>
            <AccountAssignment>
              <OrderID>900123</OrderID>
              <CostCenter>CC1000</CostCenter>
            </AccountAssignment>
          </Item>
          <Item>
            <GLAccount>200000</GLAccount>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="BRL">-150.00</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DocumentItemText>Expense Posting 02</DocumentItemText>
            <AssignmentReference>REF</AssignmentReference>
            <AccountAssignment>
              <ProfitCenter>PC1000</ProfitCenter>
            </AccountAssignment>
          </Item>
        </JournalEntry>
      </JournalEntryCreateRequest>
    </ns0:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>

The goal was to make the API easier to consume while preserving compatibility with the standard SAP interface.

Implementation in SAP Integration Suite

The transformation was implemented using standard Cloud Integration components such as Content Modifier and Message Mapping. In this specific scenario, Message Mapping was sufficient to handle the required transformations, without the need for additional Groovy scripting.

The integration flow was responsible for:

  • Receiving REST requests (JSON)
  • Transforming payloads into SOAP XML
  • Calling the standard SAP API
  • Handling responses and errors consistently

Special attention was required when handling nested structures such as account assignments, where JSON elements needed to be carefully mapped into the corresponding SOAP hierarchy.

Integration Flow

Error Handling

Error handling was a key aspect of the integration design, ensuring that technical SOAP faults were translated into clear and consistent REST responses for API consumers.

Different HTTP status codes (such as 400, 401, 403, and 500) were mapped based on the error context, providing more meaningful responses aligned with REST principles.

The following Groovy script was used within the integration flow to standardize error responses:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def status = message.getHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", Integer)
    def exceptionMessage = message.getProperty("CamelExceptionCaught")?.getMessage()    

    if (!status) {
        if (exceptionMessage?.contains("Validation")) {
            status = 400
        } else {
            status = 500
        }
    }

    message.setHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", status)
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

    def response

    switch (status.toString()) {
        case "400":
            response = """{"error":"BAD_REQUEST", "message":"Invalid payload","details":"${exceptionMessage ?: ""}"}"""
            break

        case "401":
            response = '{"error":"UNAUTHORIZED","message":"Authentication failure with backend system"}'
            break

        case "403":
            response = '{"error":"FORBIDDEN","message":"Access denied to backend system"}'
            break

        case "500":
            response = '{"error":"INTERNAL_ERROR","message":"Error processing request in backend system"}'
            break

        default:
            response = '{"error":"UNKNOWN_ERROR","message":"Unknown error"}'
    }

    message.setBody(response)

    return message;
}

In addition to technical error handling, business validation errors returned by the SAP backend were also mapped into structured JSON responses.

Example of business error response:

{
  "journalEntryResponse": {
    "documentInfo": {
      "accountingDocument": "0000000000",
      "companyCode": "1000",
      "fiscalYear": "2026"
    },
    "messages": [
      {
        "code": "RW609",
        "message": "Error in document: BKPFF (S4 system)"
      },
      {
        "code": "RW033",
        "message": "FI/CO interface: balance in transaction currency is not zero (BRL -0.01)"
      }
    ]
  }
}

This approach avoids exposing raw SOAP fault structures directly to consumers and provides a more consistent, readable, and predictable API behavior.

It also improves integration debugging by delivering structured and meaningful error messages that can be easily consumed by external systems.

Results and Benefits

This approach resulted in several improvements:

  • Easier consumption for external systems
  • Reduced payload size compared to standard SOAP structures
  • Better alignment with API-first and REST-first strategies
  • Improved maintainability and reusability

In addition, a simple comparison between SOAP and REST payloads highlighted a significant reduction in message size.

In this scenario, the SOAP payload was 2,454 bytes, while the equivalent REST payload was 740 bytes — representing a reduction of approximately 70% in message size.

SOAP vs REST payload size comparison

In high-volume scenarios, reducing message size can also contribute to lower processing and integration costs.

Design Considerations and Trade-offs

While simplifying the API, some trade-offs were considered. SOAP fault responses contain detailed technical information, which may not be fully exposed in REST responses.

This required balancing usability for external consumers with the need for sufficient diagnostic detail.

API Management Considerations

The API was exposed through SAP API Management to enable:

  • Security and authentication
  • Monitoring
  • Version control

API Management Overview

Final Thoughts

This was an interesting exercise in modernizing standard SAP APIs for broader consumption scenarios. The approach was successfully implemented in a real customer scenario, proving effective in improving integration usability.

I would be interested to hear how other teams are handling similar challenges.

👉Feel free to share your approach or exchange ideas on similar integration scenarios.

entry.title: SAP Developer News, July 9th, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS

Live from WeAreDevelopers

- WeAreDevelopers World Congress https://www.wearedevelopers.com/world-congress

CAP June 2026 release (cds 10)

- Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/2026/jun26

ABAP Cleaner in VS Code!

- https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=SAPOSS.abap-cleaner

Live streams at Code Connect next week

- Code Connect https://code-connect.dev/
- UI5con https://openui5.org/ui5con/
- UI5con agenda https://openui5.org/ui5con/program.html
- UI5con 2026 Live from the Main Stage https://www.youtube.com/live/CMPudw4scSE
- reCAP https://recap-conf.dev/
- reCAP agenda https://recap-conf.dev/program.html
- reCAP Live from the Main Stage https://broadcast.sap.com/go/reCAP
- reCAP 2026 - Streaming Sidetrack https://teams.microsoft.com/meet/347149883889697?p=ZkR4erzGjpPRtZ6zJh

Additional CAP highlights and MCP Gateway

- Building MCP Servers with SAP Integration Suite: What to Design, Not Just How to Connect https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/building-mcp-servers-with-sap-integration-suite-what-to-design-not-just-how/ba-p/14435261
- Design APIs and MCP servers https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/isuite-integrations-and-apis/design-apis?locale=en-US
- Release calendar for SAP Integration Suite https://me.sap.com/notes/3418154

Did You Know? #25 cds add test

- The cds add command https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/tools/cds-cli#cds-add

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro
00:07 Live from WeAreDevelopers
02:16 CAP June 2026 release (cds 10)
13:14 ABAP Cleaner in VS Code!
14:37 Live streams at Code Connect next week
15:05 Additional CAP highlights and MCP Gateway
17:20 DYK #25 cds add test

TRANSCRIPT

[Intro]
This is the Developer News for July 9th 2026.

[Tom, Sheena, Shilpa, Witalij]
Hi, we're coming to you this week from WeAreDevelopers conference in Berlin, which is a little bit of a difference for us developer advocates because we're actually mostly attending a conference instead of delivering one. So it's our opportunity to connect with other developers, particularly non-SAP developers from all around the world. I'm giving a session on Friday with Michael Ameling, where I'll be doing a preview of the new developers.sap.com that's all AI-powered - we'll show you more of in the coming weeks. What are the rest of you looking forward to?

Yeah we just now attended one session on Microsoft Fabric, it was really exciting, and we also got to know the latest advanced technologies that we can look forward to. So it was really exciting to be part of that session. The line was a little too long to get into the session.

That is the challenging part, to get into the sessions, but for me the interesting part is AI and agents because, it is not a surprise, but to be outside of the SAP ecosystem is what I'm interested in too, and get to learn more of what's happening outside of SAP.

So have you tried to find any sessions without AI or agents in the name?

No, all agents. I think that was the qualifier, probably. OK, and for me it's actually not the first time I'm attending this conference. I was here in 2019 and it is really good to be back here. This time SAP has a big presence here, we are actually one of the sponsors, we have our place on the show floor, we have our sessions as Thomas mentioned, he's going to do one with Michael Ameling but as well we have some other SAP colleagues delivering sessions based on the experience from their work, working with UI for example, and with user experience in this exciting AI era. So all in all, "no coffee, no code" but unfortunately the line for coffee is the longest one here, so if you don't see me coding this week, that's just because I cannot stand in this line and get my coffee.

[DJ]
cds 10 is out! That is, the next major release of the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, also known as cds 10, was published last week.

This particular major release marks many milestones and is packed full of goodness.

Within the limitations of these news items, I can only give a brief overview, and you should definitely head over to the release notes which are very readable and full of details.

Some of the features and highlights are marked as Alpha or Beta ... so please pay attention to these status badges when working through the content.

Now, like other releases, the notes for this release are organised into different categories. There's the main major release upgrade category of course, a set of important highlights, notes on changes and improvements for both the Node.js and Java runtimes, and some updates for multitenancy services as well.

So, let's start with the very essence of this major release - the upgrade and migration context, and what tht implies.

Each of the packages that make up the entire framework have had major release version upgrades.
As the release notes say, don't mix old and new major versions. Instead, embrace change and try out the new cds upgrade tool which can help you.

The runtime support is also changing, with both Node.js and Java.

And before leaving this upgrade context, I must highlight the facilities that the CAP team have put together for this essential part of the software lifecycle - upgrades and migration. There's a new Migration Guide to help you get to cds 10 ... and a bleeding edge new 'cds upgrade' facility, in Alpha, to help with upgrades.

So next, let's have a look at some of the highlights, of which there are many in this release. Some of them are either in Alpha or Beta status, but they're there!

Let me enumerate them briefly. There's now support for aggregate data in hierarchies, useful for product catalogs, org charts, bills of materials and similar scenarios. It's available right now with the Java runtime, and support for Node.js is coming soon.

For VS Code users there's a new Project Explorer to help you visually navigate through your project and model's structures.

Now, while this has been hinted at before this advent of cds 10, there's a new protocol which I'm going to describe as "CAP native", and that's now available, and it's called HCQL - CQL conveyed over HTTP. Well suited for CAP-to-CAP integrations, and available just by adding an @hcql annotation, in the true CAP tradition of magic-made-simple.

There are early AI related goodies too, in the form of CAP-level (Calesi-style) agents, and also a CAP skills library, because, as we know, focusing on intent and best practice in development is essential.

There's a new Data Inspector, which comes in the form of a plugin and allows the controlled inspection of data in the entities exposed by your services.

Now, event queues in CAP have also been given lots of love, in the form of a new scheduling API, improved processing, and an overhaul of the documentation.

Now, last December we saw the first public Beta of the 'cds export' command which we can use to create API client packages from CDS service definitions. With this cds 10 release, that command is now generally available.

Now there are some more highlights in this cds 10 release, but my good friend and colleague Antonio is going to cover those in another news item in this news episode, so stay tuned!

Let me now turn to the Node.js runtime context where there are also plenty of items for us to celebrate.

First up, consolidated service APIs. What does that mean? Well, the generic CRUD (Create Read Update Delete) handler now returns the same shape of data for all write operations. That means app, db and HCQL-proxied remote services all look and behave the same way, which is great for uniform and minimised handler code variations.

Now, AI agents are great, but one thing they don't know much about is Fiori draft data. So working with active data has been simplified and consolidated - making it easier for everyone, us humans and our AI overlords, to work in a draft-first context.

A massive piece of work that the team has undertaken over the last months, culminating in what we have with cds 10, is a significant move towards a greater embrace of modern tools and native libraries. Very briefly - the testing framework Vitest is now used in favour of Jest, which will be abandoned longer term. ECMAScript Modules (that's ESM to you and me) is the official standardised system for packages and reuse, and CAP is now embracing that as the default module system. Third party dependencies are being dropped in favour of native or built-in alternatives. For example this calendar year already has seen a move to a Node-native SQLite driver (which replaces better-sqlite3) and to the Node-native Fetch API (which replaces Axios, and in development scenarios, also removes the need for the SAP Cloud SDK).

This work to embrace modern techniques, native libraries and the newest stable standards is fantastic. An important "side effect" is also something we can celebrate - a significantly reduced set of dependencies (and thus a smaller install footprint and code surface area).

Now last but certainly not least in the Node.js category is some news on the Telemetry plugin, which is now at version 2 after a major update which also brings support for OpenTelemetry SDK 2.0.

Next, let's look at the Java runtime category, which has now reached major version 5. Lots of new features, including, importantly, support for Spring Boot 4.

There are some new minimum versions of major dependencies that you need to take care of.

With CAP Java 5 there are some changes that are not backwards compatible (as in any major release), but the migration is made easier for us with a specific migration guide and OpenRewrite recipes for automated code adjustments.

Now, there are changes to CDS property defaults to align them with recommended settings for production use.

The API methods that were deprecated in the previous major release and marked as 'forRemoval' ... have now been removed.

CAP Java 5 now defaults to JDK 25, which is a long-term-support version. The SapMachine 25 JDK with the SAP Java buildpack is recommended.

With this new major CAP Java release, search is harmonised with both CAP Node.js and RAP, and by default uses the SAP HANA search syntax (for which there is now full support). Fuzzy search is also now enabled by default on SAP HANA, with improved support for UUIDs and large strings, and available in hierarchy queries.

Now, there's also much faster typed data access available. Instead of using a dynamic proxy to handle the underlying data map for accessor interfaces, it's now possible to have implementation classes generated. This resulting in up to 10 times the performance.

Previously, CAP Java used Apache's Olingo library for OData support. Following Olingo's retirement last year, there's now built-in support for OData, that's no mean feat! That of course also means fewer dependencies and it also brings improved performance.

There's also support for creating active versions of draft-enabled entities, similar to what we learned about earlier in the Node.js runtime context.

There are also plenty more features that come with CAP Java 5. Check them out in the release notes.

But to round out the release notes overview for this cds 10 version, there are a couple of points of note in the context of MTX services. First, there's now a stricter annotation validation for extensions. And second, migration support to go from @SAP/cds-mtx to @SAP/cds-mtxs has been removed.

That pretty much wraps up things for this June 2026 major release, congratulations to the entire CAP team for a huge set of new features and improvements. And don't forget to check out Antonio's item in this news episode for those other highlights.

[Rich]
Hey friends, Rich Heilman here. I hope everyone is staying cool, staying out of the heat. Good news for ABAP developers this week. ABAP Cleaner is now available as a VS Code extension, bringing its powerful code formatting capabilities out of Eclipse and into the modern editor of choice for many developers. So, what does it do? ABAP Cleaner applies over 100 rules to your code with a single keyboard shortcut. Automatically cleaning up formatting, modernising syntax, and even flagging unused variables. The result is clean and consistent code across your entire team without anyone having to argue about style. You can run it as a quick one-shot format, or drop into interactive mode to choose exactly which rules apply to your codebase. If your team already has a configuration setup in Eclipse, you can import those rule variants directly. No need to start from scratch. The setup does a Java runtime and a bit of environment variable configuration, but once it's running, you get the same functionality that ABAP developers have relied on in ADT in Eclipse - now in a much lighter, faster editor. If you're doing ABAP development in VS Code, this one's worth installing. Check it out today.

[Ajay]
Hello everyone. Code Connect is next week, from 13th to 16th July 2026. For all of you who cannot join us in person, there are live streams available for UI5con and reCAP, from the main stage. Please check out the agenda and streaming links available for UI5con and reCAP and join us online during the Code Connect event. Links to the live streams and agendas will be available in the description.

[Antonio]
¡Hola, SAP Developers. As you already know, the June 2026 CAP release is huge. DJ has already shared many of the highlights and I just want to mention a couple more. There is a new AI plugin, in Alpha, which simplifies integration with SAP AI Core. It will automatically resolve service bindings, manage resource groups, and AI model deployments. And it provides a standard AI Core service to interact with AI Core programatically. The new Data Privacy plugin, in Beta, for Node.js, which provides out of the box integration with the SAP Data Privacy Integration Service. Based on the personal data and ILM annotations in the data model the plugin will automatically expose two endpoints that can be consumed by the service. Now, related to the integration space, CAP Node.js now supports the Event Mesh capability in SAP Integration Suite, as an alternative to the standalone SAP Event Mesh, meaning that you can now easily use it as an event broken in your CAP applications. There is the new MCP Protocol Adapter, which allows exposing existing CAP services to AI agents and LLM powered as just another protocol. Just install the plugin and annotate services with @McP, et voila, your CAP service exposes MCP functionality. Extremely powerful and easy to implement. I already used it in a CAP service that I'm developing while I was getting familiar with the new feature of SAP Integration Suite. Yes, what we've all been waiting for - the new MCP Gateway is now available as part of Integration Suite. It is part of the new Integration Cell Runtime which is a cloud native runtime purposely built for designing, deploying and operating APIs and MCP servers. It is GA now and being rolled out in the Enhanced and Premium editions of SAP Integration Suite. Not just that, but it is also available in the BTP Trial environment. What are yoiu waiting for? Go check it out. Hasta luego!

[Ajay]
Did you know that cds add does generate the best practices of the test implementation of your CAP service? One can rapidly create a sample CAP project with cds init bookshop --add sample. Run cds add test in the terminal to add test suite files for all the defined CAP services in your existing project. It creates the latest boilerplate files and structures required for you to get started with your test implementation in a jiffy. For each services it generates a sample test for the GET request for an entity and a test for any action defined in the service. This also adds the test dependencies to your project. It uses the Node test runner for the tests. Install your dependencies and just run npm t to execute your tests. The generated test implementation does need to be tweaked for the valid test data. CAP also provides a command npx cds test to mute the verbose server output and lets you focus only on the failing tests. Use -u to unmute the server output when you do not need them. Enjoy your CAP tests!

entry.title: Principal Propagation from BTP Web App to S/4HANA via SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

During a recent customer PoC, I worked on a scenario where a BTP-hosted web application needed to call an S/4HANA on-premise API — not as a generic service account, but as the actual logged-in user. 
This is where Principal Propagation becomes essential. Preserving user identity end-to-end isn't just a technical nicety — it matters for auditability, compliance, and access control. When S/4HANA receives a call as the real user, every action is traceable: security audit logs show who did what, authorizations are enforced per user, and there's no shared service account masking individual activity.

In this post, I share the configuration and lessons learned that made it work—from a BTP web application authenticated via SAP Cloud Identity Services, through SAP Integration Suite and Cloud Connector, all the way to S/4HANA, with user identity preserved throughout the entire chain.

Thanks to @cj_lo  for preparing Cloud Connector with S/HANA On-premise demo system, testing, and troubleshoot together.

Scenario

A simple web application is deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. When the user clicks a button, it triggers an iFlow on SAP Integration Suite that fetches data from S/4HANA. The S/4HANA system must receive the call as the authenticated user — not as a technical service account. 
While direct access to S/4HANA via a BTP Destination and SAP Cloud Connector is possible, many organizations choose to introduce a middleware layer to support centralized governance, security, monitoring, and error handling. Ultimately, the target architecture is customer-specific and should balance integration requirements, operational considerations, governance needs, and cost.

Browser (logged-in user)
  → BTP Web App (via Approuter + Cloud Identity Services)
    → BTP Destination Service
      → SAP Integration Suite iFlow
        → Cloud Connector
          → S/4HANA (on-premise, as the user)

Prerequisites & Scope

This article assumes the following components are already installed and configured:

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment
  • SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP Cloud Connector connected to the SAP BTP subaccount
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) as the Identity Provider
  • SAP S/4HANA system accessible through Cloud Connector
  • User identities mapped between IAS and SAP S/4HANA

This article focuses on the principal propagation scenario from a SAP BTP application through SAP Integration Suite to an on-premise SAP S/4HANA system. Topics such as SAP Cloud Connector installation, SAP BTP onboarding and trust configuration, access control setup, certificate management, and SAP S/4HANA user provisioning are outside the scope of this article. If I were to cover all of them here, I would end up writing a book instead of a mini blog 😉

Relevant SAP documentation is listed in the References section at the end of this article.

Solution Overview

The solution relies on three key components working together:

ComponentRole
ApprouterHandles IAS login, holds the user's JWT token
BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)Exchanges the user's IAS JWT for an Integration Suite-scoped token using the PI Runtime service key
iFlowReceives the user-scoped token, calls S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation

The critical insight is using OAuth2JWTBearer as the destination authentication type. This is the only type that correctly takes the user's IAS JWT and exchanges it at the PI Runtime token endpoint — preserving the user identity all the way to SAP Integration Suite.

Configuration

The BTP app consists of the following files — each is covered in the steps below:

your-app/
├── manifest.yml
├── package.json
├── xs-app.json
├── xs-security-ias.json
└── dist/
    └── index.html

Provision BTP Cloud Foundry Services

Before deploying the app, create the required service instances in your Cloud Foundry (CF) space.
Below using CF CLI, if you need a crash course using it, please check out this tutorial here.

Target your CF API endpoint and log in (in this example my region is jp10, yours could be different):

cf api https://api.cf.jp10.hana.ondemand.com
cf login

Create a Destination service instance (used by the Approuter to resolve BTP destinations), you can replace iflow-pp-destination-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service destination lite iflow-pp-destination-svc

Create an IAS (Identity) service instance using the xs-security-ias.json security descriptor, you can replace iflow-ias-svc with service name of your choice:

cf create-service identity application iflow-pp-ias-svc -c xs-security-ias.json

Where xs-security-ias.json contains:

{
  "xsappname": "iflow-pp-ias",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "credential-type": "X509_GENERATED",
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/login/callback?authType=ias"
    ],
    "post-logout-redirect-uris": [
      "https://*.cfapps.jp10.hana.ondemand.com/*/logout.html"
    ]
  }
}

credential-type: X509_GENERATED uses a certificate-based credential instead of a client secret — recommended for production.

Both service instances are bound to the app via manifest.yml:

applications:
  - name: poc-iflow-pp-fe
    memory: 512M
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    services:
      - iflow-pp-ias-svc
      - iflow-pp-destination-svc

Ensure the User's Email Matches S/4HANA Identity

The Cloud Connector Subject Pattern (configured under Cloud To On-Premise → Principal Propagation) determines how the X.509 certificate CN is built. The default pattern is:

CN=${name}

${name} is sourced from the user_name claim in the PI Runtime token. During the OAuth2JWTBearer exchange, PI Runtime populates user_name from the email claim of the incoming IAS JWT — not from sub or any other claim.

So the identity that reaches Cloud Connector and ultimately S/4HANA is always the user's email address.
This behaviour is important because many troubleshooting efforts focus on the IAS sub claim, while principal propagation in this flow ultimately depends on the user's email address being propagated and resolvable by the backend system.

Important: Make sure the user's email address in IAS matches a user identity that S/4HANA can resolve — either via direct email-to-username mapping on the S/4 side, or as the logon name if S/4 uses email as the logon name format.
Depending on your identity governance requirements, the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern can use mapping logic beyond a basic CN. On the S/4HANA side, certificate-to-user mapping (CERTRULE) adds a second layer of control, allowing a certificate CN to be mapped to a different logon name. For complex scenarios, refer to the Cloud Connector Subject Pattern Syntax and S/4HANA Certificate-Based User Mapping documentation to design patterns that fit your enterprise architecture.

To verify what identity Cloud Connector is propagating, check CC Admin UI → Logging and Tracing — the outbound request will show the CN value used in the short-lived certificate.

Here's how my configuration looks like in Cloud Connector:

 Here's how rule-based certificate mapping looks like in S/4HANA - /ncertrule

Create Process Integration Runtime Service Instance

You probably has setup this one in order to trigger any published iFlow; or if you have one but has not included below grant type, then you can create a new one in BTP Cockpit → Service Marketplace → Process Integration Runtime:

  • Plan: integration-flow
  • Grant Types to enable:
    • client_credentials (for testing)
    • jwt bearer (the one that we need)
    • refresh_token
    • saml2 bearer
  • Create a Service Key and note down clientid, clientsecret, and tokenurl

Why PI Runtime and not IAS credentials? In my landscape setup, SAP Integration Suite only trusts tokens issued by its own OAuth authorization server (via the PI Runtime instance - current the SAP Integration Suite is authenticated via SAP Default idP). IAS tokens forwarded directly are not trusted by SAP Integration Suite unless full IAS-to-Integration Suite trust federation is set up. The PI Runtime service key is the standard supported path.

Configure BTP Destination

In BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination:

FieldValue
Name<unique destination name, i.e. m25_iflow>
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://<your-cpi-host>/http/<your-iflow-endpoint>
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2JWTBearer
Client ID<clientid from PI Runtime service key>
Client Secret<clientsecret from PI Runtime service key>
Token Service URL<tokenurl from PI Runtime service key>

No additional properties required.

Common pitfalls:

  • Do not use OAuth2UserTokenExchange — it uses refresh_token grant internally which PI Runtime does not support
  • Do not use IAS credentials here — SAP Integration Suite will reject them
  • The destination name must exactly match what is referenced in xs-app.json (watch for underscore vs hyphen)

BTP App — Approuter Setup

The frontend app is a plain HTML file served by the SAP Approuter (@sap/approuter). No custom middleware needed.

package.json:

{
  "name": "ar-iflow-pp-fe",
  "dependencies": {
    "@sap/approuter": "^22.0.3"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node node_modules/@sap/approuter/approuter.js"
  }
}

xs-app.json — routes inbound requests to the BTP destination or local static files:
Also remember to double-check the Destination name here in case you're using different name.

{
  "welcomeFile": "/index.html",
  "authenticationMethod": "route",
  "logout": { "logoutEndpoint": "/logout" },
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^/user-api(.*)",
      "target": "$1",
      "service": "sap-approuter-userapi"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/m25_iflow(.*)$",
      "destination": "m25_iflow",
      "csrfProtection": false,
      "target": "$1",
      "authenticationType": "ias"
    },
    {
      "source": "^/(.*)$",
      "localDir": "dist",
      "authenticationType": "ias",
      "cacheControl": "no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate"
    }
  ]
}

The /user-api route uses the built-in Approuter user info service — no custom code required to read the logged-in user's details.

dist/index.html — the entire frontend in one file:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <title>Principal Propagation PoC</title>
  <style>
    body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 40px auto; padding: 0 16px; }
    button { padding: 8px 20px; margin-top: 8px; cursor: pointer; }
    pre { background: #f4f4f4; padding: 12px; white-space: pre-wrap; word-break: break-all; margin-top: 8px; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>

<h2>Principal Propagation PoC</h2>

<h3>Logged-in User</h3>
  <pre id="userInfo">Loading...</pre>

<h3>Trigger iFlow</h3>
  <button onclick="callIflow()">Call iFlow (GET /m25_iflow)</button>
  <pre id="iflowResponse">Response will appear here</pre>

<script>
    // Fetch logged-in user info from built-in Approuter user API
    fetch("/user-api/currentUser")
      .then(r => r.json())
      .then(u => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = JSON.stringify(u, null, 2))
      .catch(e => document.getElementById("userInfo").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);

// Trigger iFlow via BTP Destination (OAuth2JWTBearer)
    function callIflow() {
      call("/m25_iflow");
    }

function call(url) {
      document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Calling " + url + "...";
      fetch(url, {
        headers: { "X-Requested-With": "XMLHttpRequest" }
      })
        .then(async r => {
          const text = await r.text();
          try { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(text), null, 2); } catch (_) { return "HTTP " + r.status + "\n\n" + text; }
        })
        .then(body => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = body)
        .catch(e => document.getElementById("iflowResponse").textContent = "Error: " + e.message);
    }
  </script>

</body>
</html>

Deploy:

cf push

Assign ESBMessaging.send Role to Users (IAS Group Mapping)

In BTP Cockpit → Security → Role Collections → Create (e.g. CPIMessagingSend) :

Roles tab — add:

Application IdentifierRole TemplateRole
it-rt-<your-instance>ESBMessagingESBMessaging.send

User Groups tab — add:

Identity ProviderName
<your-ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com — Business UsersIntegration_Senders

Make sure your users are members of the Integration_Senders group in IAS, and select Business Users (not Application Users) as the user type — logged-in app users are business users in IAS.

iFlow configuration summary: The iFlow uses an HTTPS Sender Adapter with User Role authorization (ESBMessaging.send) and an OData/HTTP Receiver Adapter configured for On-Premise proxy type with Principal Propagation authentication. See the Integration Flow section below for the full configuration.

Building Integration Flow

This section assumes you are familiar with creating integration flows in Cloud Integration of SAP Integration Suite.

The integration flow in this demo scenario is intentionally minimal — its purpose is to receive the inbound call from the BTP web app, forward it to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector with Principal Propagation, and return the response.

The iFlow contains the following steps:

  1. HTTPS Sender Adapter — receives the inbound HTTP request from the BTP Approuter
  2. Request-Reply step — forwards the call to the S/4HANA OData service
  3. OData V2 Receiver Adapter — calls S/4HANA on-premise via Cloud Connector

HTTPS Sender Adapter configuration:

FieldValue
Address/your-iflow-endpoint
AuthorizationUser Role
User RoleESBMessaging.send

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Connection tab:

FieldValue
Addresshttp://<virtual-host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>
Proxy TypeOn-Premise
AuthenticationPrincipal Propagation
Location ID<your Cloud Connector location ID>
Custom Query Parametersap-client=<client no.>

OData V2 Receiver Adapter — Processing tab:

FieldValue
OperationQuery (GET)
Resource Path<EntitySetName>
Query Options$top=10 (or as needed)
Custom Query Options<SAP Client Number>

The sap-client parameter must go in Custom Query Parameters (Connection tab). The Resource Path must have no leading slash — e.g. `A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`, not `/A_GLAccountInChartOfAccounts`.

Testing the Flow

With the web app deployed, open it in the browser. You will see the logged-in user's identity displayed (via the /user-api/currentUser endpoint exposed by the Approuter). Click Call via iFlow (GET /m25_iflow) button to trigger the integration flow.

A successful end-to-end flow will show the same user email across the BTP app, the Integration Suite message log, and the S/4HANA audit log. The sections below describe how to verify each layer.

Cloud Connector — Trace

If the call reaches the Cloud Connector but something goes wrong on the S/4HANA side, enable tracing in the Cloud Connector Admin UI:

  1. Go to Cloud Connector Admin UI → Logs and Trace → Trace (Debug level)
  2. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  3. View the trace (scc_core.trc) and search for the CN value — this confirms which user identity Cloud Connector placed in the short-lived X.509 certificate sent to S/4HANA

This is especially useful when the call appears to succeed from the Integration Suite side but S/4HANA returns a 401 or an authorization error — it lets you confirm the CN matches a user S/4HANA can resolve.

S/4HANA — ICF Trace (`/nsectrace`)

For the deepest level of verification, enable ICF tracing on the S/4HANA side to see exactly what the system receives at the HTTP layer:

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction /nsectrace
  2. Navigate to ICF Service / Path and enable the trace for the relevant ICF service path (e.g. `/sap/opu/odata/sap/<service-name>`) and click Activate Trace
  3. Trigger the iFlow again from the web app
  4. Back in /nsectrace, click Deactivate Trace, and choose Show Trace — the trace output will show the inbound request headers including the ssl-client-subject field, which contains the CN from the Cloud Connector certificate

The CN value here is what S/4HANA evaluates for user mapping. If it does not match a known logon name, the call will fail with a 401 even if everything upstream is correct.

Key Takeaways

Principal Propagation is more than a technical configuration—it enables auditability, compliance, and least-privilege access control by ensuring S/4HANA receives requests as the actual business user rather than a shared service account.

The implementation approach depends on customer requirements and the overall solution architecture. Whether user identity is propagated through direct connectivity or a middleware layer, the objective remains the same: maintain end-to-end user context while balancing integration needs, governance, operational considerations, and cost.

One of the key lessons learned is that principal propagation depends on several components being configured consistently across BTP, Integration Suite, Cloud Connector, and S/4HANA. Even small trust or authorization gaps can prevent user identity from being propagated successfully, making end-to-end validation essential.

References

The following SAP documentation was useful while implementing and troubleshooting this scenario.

SAP BTP - Cloud Foundry Environment

  • Administration and Operations in the Cloud Foundry

SAP Cloud Connector and S/4HANA (ABAP)

  • Cloud Connector Documentation
  • Installation Guide
  • Managing Subaccounts
  • Principal Propagation Configuration
  • Configure Access Control (HTTP)
  • Configure Subject Patterns
  • Subject Pattern Rules API
  • S/4HANA / ABAP – Rule-Based Certificate Mapping (CERTRULE)

SAP Integration Suite

  • SAP Integration Suite Documentation
  • Cloud Integration Documentation

SAP Cloud Identity Services

  • Identity Authentication (IAS) Documentation
  • Destination Service Documentation
  • OAuth2JWTBearer Authentication

entry.title: Creating an MCP Server in SAP Integration Suite (Trial) – A Hands-on Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Integration Suite now provides an option to create and expose an MCP Server from within the Integration Suite environment. I recently tested this feature in an SAP BTP trial account and wanted to share a short, practical walkthrough based on my hands-on experience.
