The goal of this blog is simple: show how to create, publish, and test an MCP Server using SAP Integration Suite in a trial setup.

More details about available Integration Suite service plans and capabilities can be found in SAP Note 2903776 - SAP Integration Suite – Service Plans and Upgrade Paths

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure you have:

  • An active SAP BTP trial account
  • Access to SAP Integration Suite 
  • Required capabilities activated, especially:
    • Integration Cell
  • A test API or service that you want to expose
  • Basic understanding of APIs and SAP BTP cockpit navigation

Step 1: Enable the Required Capability

  1. Inside Integration Suite, go to Settings → Runtimes
  2. Check if the Integration Cell runtime is available (if not, please wait until your tenant has it)
  3. Enable the feature ~ takes a while but works (if not already active)

Step 2: Create MCP Server Configuration

Go to an existing or new Integration Package.
Create a new artifact of type: MCP Server

Then maintain the basic configuration:

  • Select the source type
    • In this example: API
  • Provide the required details
  • Ensure that the MCP path is unique
  • Select the required tools based on the API resources
  • Save the artifact

After the artifact is created:

  • Open the MCP configuration
  • Add the required resources
  • Save it as a version and Deploy the MCP Server
  • Select the runtime and virtual host
Once deployment is successful, the MCP Server is active.
 
 

 

Step 3: Publish MCP Server in Developer Hub

After deployment, the MCP Server needs to be published so that it can be discovered and consumed.

Go to Developer Hub and add new content.
Then:

  • Select the relevant business system
  • Choose the Integration Suite business system
  • Go to MCP Servers
  • Select the MCP Server you want to publish
  • Create a product
  • Publish the product
  • Subscribe to the product to get the required credentials

This step is important because the client tool needs connection details and credentials to access the MCP Server.


Step 4: Testing the MCP Server

For testing, I used Cline as the AI tool.
The high-level steps were:

  • Add the newly created MCP Server to the AI tool
  • Maintain the server URL and credentials (basic auth - enabled in base API)
  • Make sure the server is active and allowed
  • Ask a question that should trigger the tool call
  • Validate the response from the MCP Server

In my test, the AI tool was able to call the MCP Server and receive a response successfully.

Personal Experience

Overall, the setup was quite straightforward once the required runtime was available.

What worked well

  • MCP Server artifact creation is integrated into the familiar Integration Suite package model.
  • Publishing through Developer Hub makes the server easier to consume.
  • Tool selection based on API resources is simple to understand.
  • Testing from an AI tool like Cline gives quick validation.

Key observations

  • The MCP path must be unique, so naming needs attention.
  • Credentials and server configuration in the AI client must be maintained carefully.
  • For first testing, it is better to start with a simple API with basic auth enabled.

Conclusion

The MCP Server feature in SAP Integration Suite is a useful step toward exposing enterprise APIs for AI-based consumption in a controlled way.

From my hands-on trial account experience, the setup flow is understandable and can be tested end-to-end with a simple API and an MCP-compatible client.

For anyone exploring AI integration scenarios with SAP Integration Suite, this is a good feature to try in a trial environment first

References

  • 2903776 - SAP Integration Suite – Service Plans and Upgrade Paths
  • API-Centric Integration | SAP Help Portal
  • Model Context Protocol (MCP) | SAP Help Portal
  • Create an MCP Server from an API Artifact | SAP Help Portal
  • Limits in API-Centric Integration | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 1: Build and Deploy Your API
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Integration Suite provides a unified environment for connecting applications, data, and business processes across cloud and on-premise landscapes. As organizations increasingly expose business capabilities through APIs, APIs are no longer just interfaces to backend systems—they have become a fundamental building block for enterprise integration.

API-centric integration brings API design and integration capabilities together in a unified experience. It enables developers to create APIs that directly connect to backend systems, mediate and transform requests and responses, compose multiple services, and expose simplified interfaces to API consumers. With this approach, developers can build APIs ranging from simple managed endpoints to rich integration scenarios while applying security, traffic management, and lifecycle governance as part of the same development experience.

This blog is the first part of a two-part series that walks through the complete API lifecycle on the Integration Cell runtime.

In Part 1, you will activate the required capabilities and the Integration Cell runtime, create your first API Artifact, explore its design and runtime components, deploy it, and verify the deployment using the monitoring console.

In Part 2, we will explore Developer Hub, the centralized catalog in SAP Integration Suite for publishing and discovering APIs, Events, and MCP Servers. You will learn how to bundle the deployed API into an API Product, publish it for discovery, create and subscribe a developer application, obtain application credentials, and securely invoke the published API.

Across both blogs, you will complete the following end-to-end journey:

Activate Integration Cell → Design API → Deploy → Publish → Subscribe → Consume

Understanding the API-Centric Integration

Traditional API management often starts with an existing backend service and exposes it through a managed API endpoint. API-centric integration extends this model by allowing integration capabilities to be designed directly as part of the API.

An API can therefore do more than simply proxy requests to a backend system. Depending on the business scenario, it can connect to backend services, transform and mediate messages, combine data from multiple sources, and expose a simplified interface tailored to the needs of API consumers.

API-centric integration supports scenarios such as:

  • API proxying — expose an existing backend service through a secure and managed API endpoint.
  • API mediation — transform or enrich requests and responses between API consumers and backend systems.
  • API simplification — hide backend complexity and expose a simpler, consumer-friendly API contract.

These capabilities are brought together through a unified API modelling experience. Developers can design the API contract and configure the required integration logic as part of the same API Artifact, then deploy it to the Integration Cell runtime.

For a simple API, the runtime flow can be represented as:

API Consumer → API Artifact → Backend Service

For more advanced scenarios, the API Artifact can contain the integration logic required to transform, mediate, or compose backend services before returning a response to the consumer. In the following sections, we will start with the simplest API-centric integration scenario: creating an API Artifact that exposes a backend service through a managed API endpoint on Integration Cell.

 

Prerequisites

Getting started with API Management on Integration Cell involves enabling the runtime and using the unified design environment to create, deploy, and manage API Artifacts.

Before proceeding, ensure that:

  • Your SAP Integration Suite tenant is configured and the API Management capability is activated.
  • Integration Cell runtime activation
  • You have the required role collections assigned for provisioning, designing and subscription across Integration Suite and Developer Hub applications.

With these prerequisites in place, the end-to-end journey involves four main steps:

  1. Enable API Management and configure access to Developer Hub.
  2. Activate Integration Cell from the tenant settings.
  3. Design and deploy APIs using the unified modelling experience.
  4. Create an application in Developer Hub, subscribe to the API product, and consume the API.

Activate the Integration Cell Runtime

Before you can design and deploy API Artifacts to Integration Cell, you need to activate the API Management capability and activate the Integration Cell runtime. Make sure you have Integration_Provisioner role collection assigned.

Navigation:
SAP Integration Suite → Home → Manage Capabilities

In the Select Capabilities step, select Manage APIs and choose Next.

In the API Management configuration step:

  • API Modelling is selected by default.
  • Select Developer Hub to enable API discovery and consumption.

Choose Next.

Review the selected capabilities in the Summary step and choose Activate.

 

Once the activation is complete, API Modelling is available for designing API Artifacts, and Developer Hub can be used for API discovery and consumption.

Activate the Integration Cell Runtime

After enabling the API Management capability, navigate to runtime management.

Navigation:
SAP Integration Suite → Settings → Runtimes → Integration Cell

The Integration Cell tab displays the current status of the runtime. If the runtime has not yet been activated, its status is shown as Inactive. Choose Activate to start provisioning the Integration Cell runtime.

Integration Cell is a fully managed runtime, so no Kubernetes cluster or infrastructure configuration is required from the user. The underlying runtime environment is provisioned and managed by SAP.

Verify the Runtime

After activation is complete, return to:

SAP Integration Suite → Settings → Runtimes → Integration Cell

Verify that the Integration Cell status is shown as Active.

The runtime is now ready for API Artifact deployment.

Before proceeding to API modelling, assign the PI_Integration_Developer role collection. This role collection provides the required access to design and deploy API Artifacts to Integration Cell.

Artifacts Supported on Integration Cell
SAP Integration Suite enables you to design and manage two types of artifacts for deployment to Integration Cell: 

API enable you to expose backend services through managed API endpoints. They provide capabilities for securing APIs, managing traffic, transforming and mediating requests and responses, and governing APIs throughout their lifecycle.

MCP server enable you to expose enterprise capabilities and data through the Model Context Protocol (MCP), making them accessible to AI-powered applications and agents.

In this article, we will focus on the API Artifact and walk through the process of creating, deploying, publishing, and consuming it on Integration Cell.

Create Your First API Artifact

With the Integration Cell runtime activated and the content package in place, you are ready to create your first API Artifact. API Artifacts are created within a content package, allowing related APIs and integration assets to be organized and managed together.

Navigate to the  content package in this case Product and Inventory Services and choose Artifacts > Add > API.

Step 1: Select the Runtime Profile

The first step is to choose the runtime profile where the API Artifact will be deployed. The selected runtime profile determines the capabilities available for the API Artifact.

Select Integration Cell as the runtime profile and choose Next.

 

Step 2: Select the API Creation Method

The next step is to choose how you want to create the API Artifact. Integration Cell supports multiple creation methods to address different integration scenarios.

  • URL or Specification – Create an API by providing a backend URL, importing an API specification/metadata in json/yaml/edmx format or uploading an API zip bundle.
  • API Provider – Create an API by discovering services from a backend system or by using a configured destination.
  • Reusable API – Create an API that invokes another API Artifact using the API Direct adapter, enabling modular and reusable API-centric integration scenarios.

For this blog, select URL or Specification and choose Next.

Step 3: Provide the API Details

In the final step, configure the details of the API Artifact. Depending on your use case, you can create the API Artifact in one of two ways:

  • Create – Generate an API Artifact by providing the URL of the backend service. The API definition is discovered from the backend endpoint.
  • Upload – Create an API Artifact by importing an existing API definition in YAML/JSON, EDMX, or an API ZIP bundle.

For this tutorial, we will use the Upload option and import an OpenAPI specification.

The uploaded OpenAPI specification references an existing SAP BTP Destination named S4Hana_Cloud_Edition.

During deployment and runtime, Integration Cell uses this destination to securely connect to the backend system without exposing backend connection details in the API Artifact.

The destination defines the backend host, authentication details, and connectivity configuration required to access the SAP S/4HANA Cloud system. Multiple API artifacts can reuse the same destination, enabling centralized management of backend connectivity.

Next, import the OpenAPI specification and verify that the destination referenced in the specification is resolved automatically.

Complete the remaining API details:

  • Target: S4Hana_Cloud_Edition (Destination)
  • Name: S4Hana Purchase Order
  • ID: S4Hana_Purchase_order
  • Service Type: OData
  • API Base Path: /purchaseorders
  • API State: Active

Choose Add to create the API Artifact.

The API artifact is created and opens in the API Designer, where you can review the generated API definition, configure policies, and prepare it for deployment to the Integration Cell runtime. API Designer provides a unified environment for configuring the API definition, runtime flow, target connectivity, and policies.

Multiple API artifacts can reuse the same destination, enabling centralized management of backend connectivity.

Explore the API Artifact

The API Artifact has now been created and is ready for review before deployment. The artifact provides a consolidated view of the API configuration, backend connectivity, exposed resources, and request processing flow. Understanding these components helps you validate the API and determine where additional configuration, such as policies or transformations, can be applied.

Overview

The Overview tab provides the general information about the API Artifact, including its name, identifier, version, runtime profile, API base path, lifecycle state, and deployment status. It also displays the generated API URL and metadata such as creation and modification details.

This page serves as the central summary of the API Artifact and is useful for verifying its overall configuration.

 

Target Endpoint

The Target Endpoint tab defines where the API forwards incoming requests.

Since the API was created by importing an OpenAPI specification that references an SAP BTP Destination, the artifact automatically resolves the configured destination (S4Hana_Cloud_Edition) along with the relative service path. During runtime, Integration Cell combines the destination URL with the relative path to invoke the backend service.

Using destinations externalizes backend connectivity and credentials from the API Artifact, making the API easier to transport across landscapes while keeping connection details centrally managed.

 

Resources

The Resources tab lists all resources and operations that are exposed by the API Artifact. These resources are generated from the imported API definition and include the supported HTTP methods and corresponding resource paths.

For OData services, the resources represent the exposed entity sets and operations defined by the backend service.

Selecting Open Editor launches the visual API editor, where you can modify resources, update the API definition, add or remove operations, and configure the request processing flow.

Policies

When an API Artifact is created with the specific service type, SAP Integration Suite automatically applies a default processing template. This template provides the runtime flow required to receive, authenticate, authorize, and forward API requests to the backend service.

The default processing flow consists of the following components:

  • OData V2 Sender Adapter
  • Authentication Policy
  • Authorization Policy
  • OData Receiver Adapter

 

OData V2 Sender Adapter

The OData V2 Sender Adapter acts as the entry point for the API Artifact. It exposes the managed OData endpoint that API consumers invoke and forwards incoming requests to the policy model, where the configured security and governance policies are executed before the request is routed to the backend service.

Authentication Policy

The Authentication policy is the first policy executed in the processing flow and is mandatory for every API Artifact. It verifies the identity of the API consumer before allowing the request to proceed. The policy supports multiple authentication mechanisms, including Basic Authentication, OAuth 2.0, and Client Certificate authentication. For newly created API Artifacts, it also supports External OAuth (OIDC), enabling token validation from external identity providers such as Microsoft Entra ID, Google, and Okta. Multiple authentication methods can be enabled simultaneously, allowing APIs to support different client applications and security requirements.

 

Authorization Policy

After a request is successfully authenticated, the Authorization policy determines whether the authenticated caller is permitted to invoke the API. It evaluates access based on OAuth Scopes, Developer Keys, or a combination of both, providing flexible authorization for different API consumption scenarios.

The policy supports the following authorization modes:

  • OAuth Scope or Developer Key (Default) – Access is granted if the caller presents either the required OAuth scope or a valid Developer Key.
  • OAuth Scope and Developer Key – Both a valid OAuth scope and a Developer Key are required.
  • OAuth Scope Only – Access is granted only if the access token contains the configured OAuth scope.
  • Developer Key Only – Access is granted only to subscribed applications presenting a valid Developer Key.

For OAuth-based authorization, the policy validates the configured Scope Key (JWT claim) and the required Scope value. By default, the scope is set to API.invoke, ensuring that only callers with the appropriate permission can invoke the API.

OData Receiver Adapter

Once all configured policies have been successfully executed, the OData Receiver Adapter forwards the request to the backend OData service using the configured destination. It manages the backend connectivity and returns the response through the same processing pipeline to the API consumer.

Extending the Policy Flow

Beyond the default template, SAP Integration Suite provides a rich set of policies that can be added to the processing flow to address different API management requirements. Common policy categories include:

  • Security – Authenticate and authorize API consumers, validate requests, and protect APIs against malicious or malformed traffic.
  • Traffic Management – Apply policies such as Quota and Spike Arrest to control API consumption and protect backend systems.
  • Transformation and Mediation – Modify request and response payloads, convert data formats, enrich messages, invoke external services, or execute JavaScript and Python scripts as part of the API flow.

For this blog, we will keep the default policy configuration unchanged and proceed with deploying the API Artifact to the Integration Cell runtime. Subsequent blogs in this series will explore how to configure and use these policies to build secure, resilient, and production-ready APIs.

Deploy the API Artifact

After reviewing the API Artifact and its configuration, the next step is to deploy it to the Integration Cell runtime. Deployment publishes the API Artifact to the selected runtime, making it available for invocation through the configured API endpoint.

To deploy the API, click the Deploy icon from the API Designer toolbar. In the deployment dialog, verify that Integration Cell is selected as the Runtime Profile, choose the appropriate Virtual Host, and click Yes to start the deployment.

 

Once the deployment is complete, the API status changes from Not Deployed to Deployed, and the generated API endpoint becomes active on the selected Virtual Host. You can now invoke the API directly or publish it through an API Product for controlled discovery and consumption using Developer Hub.

For any subsequent changes to the API Artifact, save the changes and redeploy the artifact to make the updated configuration effective on the Integration Cell runtime.

 

 

Monitor the Deployed API Artifact

Once the API Artifact has been successfully deployed, you can monitor its runtime status and operational details from the Monitor view in SAP Integration Suite.

Navigation:
Monitor → Integrations and APIs

The Overview page provides a consolidated view of all integration content deployed to the selected runtime profile. It displays runtime health, message processing statistics, deployment status, and operational metrics, allowing you to quickly assess the overall state of your Integration Cell runtime.

To view details of a specific deployment, select the API Artifact from the Manage Integration Content section.


The details page displays comprehensive runtime information about the deployed artifact, including:

  • Deployment Status – Indicates whether the artifact is successfully deployed and running.
  • Endpoints – Displays the runtime endpoint through which the API can be invoked.
  • Artifact Details – Provides deployment metadata and quick navigation to the Artifact Editor or message monitoring.
  • Status Details – Shows the current deployment state and any deployment-related messages.
  • Download and Undeploy – Allows you to download the deployed artifact or remove it from the runtime.

This view serves as the primary operational console for managing deployed API Artifacts on the Integration Cell runtime.

 Log Level

For troubleshooting and diagnostics, the runtime log level of an API Artifact can be configured directly from the monitoring view.

Supported log levels include:

  • None – Disables runtime logging.
  • Info – Records high-level execution events and is recommended for normal operation.
  • Error – Logs only error conditions and exceptions.
  • Debug – Captures detailed execution information for in-depth troubleshooting.

The selected log level controls the amount of diagnostic information captured during API execution and can be adjusted as needed without modifying or redeploying the API Artifact. This capability simplifies production support and helps isolate runtime issues while minimizing operational overhead.

Conclusion (End of Part 1)

In this blog, you activated the Integration Cell runtime, created your first API Artifact, explored its design and runtime components, deployed it successfully, and verified its deployment using the monitoring console.

At this stage, the API is available on the Integration Cell runtime but is not yet discoverable by application developers. The next step is to govern its consumption by publishing it through Developer Hub.

In the next blog, we shift our focus from API design and deployment to API governance and controlled consumption through Developer Hub. We will explore how Developer Hub acts as the centralized catalog for APIs, Events, and MCP Servers. You will learn how to create an API Product, publish it for discovery, create and subscribe a developer application, obtain application credentials, and securely invoke the published API. By the end of the second blog, you will have completed the complete API lifecycle—from design and deployment to governed publication and secure consumption.

entry.title: Laptop Health Monitoring System
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community, In this blog post, we will explore how to Monitor Laptop Health Using SAP Integration Suite and Send Automated Email Alerts.

What is the Problem?
In most companies, employee laptops keep generating signals like high CPU temperature, battery drain, or device issues. But the problem is, no one is really tracking all this data properly in real time. Because of that, issues are usually noticed only when something goes wrong and the user raises a complaint.

What Are We Building?
To solve this, we are creating a system that automatically monitors device health data. For now, we are starting with static data to build and test the solution. The data flows into SAP CPI, where it is checked against certain conditions. If something looks wrong, the system will either send an alert email to the user without any manual effort.

Why Does It Matter?
If these issues are detected early, it can save a lot of time and avoid bigger problems. It helps users work smoothly without interruptions and allows IT teams to act faster. Overall, it makes the whole support process more proactive instead of reactive.

 

In this blog, I’ll explain the whole process step by step in a simple way. Let’s go through it together!
 
iFlow Overview :
We start by creating a simple integration flow in SAP CPI.

This flow will:

  • Receive device data from Postman
  • Process and validate the data
  • Send an email alert based on device health

The complete flow looks like this:

 

Step 1: Input from Postman

We will send JSON data from Postman. For now, we are using static data for testing.

Let's take data for three devices, You can add more records as needed.

{
  "devices": [
    {
      "employee_id": "EMP001",
      "employee_name": "User One",
      "employee_email": "user1@example.com",
      "department": "IT",
      "location": "Hyderabad",

      "device_id": "SAFE001",
      "brand": "Dell",
      "ModelName": "Dell Safe Model",
      "model_year": 2023,
      "os": "Windows 11",

      "usage_type": "Office",
      "daily_usage_hours": 6,
      "CPUUsage": 40,

      "RAMUsage": 45,
      "Temperature": 45,
      "Voltage": 12,
      "DiskUsage": 40,
      "FanSpeed": 2000,

      "battery_health_percent": 95,
      "charging_cycles": 200,
      "battery_age_months": 12,
      "avg_charge_limit_percent": 70,

      "screen_condition": "Good",
      "port_status": "Working",
      "mouse_status": "Working",
      "keyboard_status": "AllWorking",
      "keyboard_backlight": "Working",
      "speaker_status": "Working",
      "mic_status": "Working",
      "camera_status": "Working"
    },
    {
      "employee_id": "EMP002",
      "employee_name": "User Two",
      "employee_email": "user2@example.com",
      "department": "IT",
      "location": "Hyderabad",

      "device_id": "UNSAFE001",
      "brand": "HP",
      "ModelName": "HP Critical Model",
      "model_year": 2015,
      "os": "Windows 10",

      "usage_type": "Gaming",
      "daily_usage_hours": 15,

      "CPUUsage": 97,
      "RAMUsage": 95,
      "Temperature": 100,
      "Voltage": 9,
      "DiskUsage": 98,
      "FanSpeed": 7000,

      "battery_health_percent": 20,
      "charging_cycles": 1200,
      "battery_age_months": 72,
      "avg_charge_limit_percent": 100,

      "screen_condition": "Broken",
      "port_status": "NotWorking",
      "mouse_status": "NotWorking",
      "keyboard_status": "NotWorking",
      "keyboard_backlight": "NotWorking",
      "speaker_status": "NotWorking",
      "mic_status": "NotWorking",
      "camera_status": "NotWorking"
    },
    {
      "employee_id": "EMP003",
      "employee_name": "User Three",
      "employee_email": "user3@example.com",
      "department": "Support",
      "location": "Bangalore",

      "device_id": "MIXED001",
      "brand": "Lenovo",
      "ModelName": "Lenovo Mixed Model",
      "model_year": 2019,
      "os": "Windows 11",

      "usage_type": "Gaming",
      "daily_usage_hours": 12,

      "CPUUsage": 75,
      "RAMUsage": 65,
      "Temperature": 72,
      "Voltage": 10.5,
      "DiskUsage": 85,
      "FanSpeed": 4500,

      "battery_health_percent": 65,
      "charging_cycles": 650,
      "battery_age_months": 36,
      "avg_charge_limit_percent": 90,

      "screen_condition": "Flickering",
      "port_status": "Loose",
      "mouse_status": "Lagging",
      "keyboard_status": "FewKeysIssue",
      "keyboard_backlight": "Dim",
      "speaker_status": "LowSound",
      "mic_status": "Noise",
      "camera_status": "Blur"
    }
  ]
}

Step 2: HTTPS Sender

Configure HTTPS adapter.

This is the entry point of the integration flow.

Step 3: JSON to XML

Convert JSON into XML.

Step 4: General Splitter

Split multiple device records. So it will Process one device at a time. 

Step 5: Groovy Script

Now comes the main part — the Groovy script.

Here we:

  • Read device values
  • Apply validation logic
  • Assign severity levels (Safe, Risky, Critical, etc.)
  • Prepare issue list
  • Build HTML output for email.

Groovy Script:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def body = message.getBody(java.io.Reader)
    def xml = new XmlSlurper().parse(body)

    def issues = []
    def alertFlag = false

    // SAFE FUNCTION
    def getVal = { field ->
        try {
            xml.devices[0]?."${field}"?.text()?.trim()
        } catch(Exception e) {
            null
        }
    }

    def toInt = { val ->
        try {
            val ? val.toInteger() : null
        } catch(Exception e) {
            null
        }
    }

    // RANGE VALIDATOR
    def evaluateRange = { field, value, ranges ->

        if (value == null) return

        for (r in ranges) {

            if (value >= r.min && value <= r.max) {

                issues.add("${field} → ${r.status} (${value})")

                if (["Unsafe", "Very Risky", "Critical"].contains(r.status)) {
                    alertFlag = true
                }

                return
            }
        }

        issues.add("${field} → Critical (Invalid Value: ${value})")
        alertFlag = true
    }

    // ENUM VALIDATOR
    def evaluateEnum = { field, value, mapping ->

        if (value == null) return

        def status = mapping[value]

        if (status) {

            issues.add("${field} → ${status} (${value})")

            if (["Unsafe", "Very Risky", "Critical"].contains(status)) {
                alertFlag = true
            }

        } else {

            issues.add("${field} → Critical (Invalid Input: ${value})")
            alertFlag = true
        }
    }

    // FETCH VALUES
    def cpu = toInt(getVal("CPUUsage"))
    def ram = toInt(getVal("RAMUsage"))
    def temp = toInt(getVal("Temperature"))
    def voltage = toInt(getVal("Voltage"))
    def disk = toInt(getVal("DiskUsage"))
    def fan = toInt(getVal("FanSpeed"))

    def batteryHealth = toInt(getVal("battery_health_percent"))
    def cycles = toInt(getVal("charging_cycles"))
    def batteryAge = toInt(getVal("battery_age_months"))

    def dailyUsage = toInt(getVal("daily_usage_hours"))
    def usageType = getVal("usage_type")

    def screen = getVal("screen_condition")
    def port = getVal("port_status")
    def mouse = getVal("mouse_status")
    def keyboard = getVal("keyboard_status")
    def backlight = getVal("keyboard_backlight")
    def speaker = getVal("speaker_status")
    def mic = getVal("mic_status")
    def camera = getVal("camera_status")

    def emp_email = getVal("employee_email")

    // VALIDATIONS
    evaluateRange("CPU Usage", cpu, [
        [min:0, max:50, status:"Safe"],
        [min:51, max:70, status:"Normal"],
        [min:71, max:80, status:"Moderate"],
        [min:81, max:90, status:"Risky"],
        [min:91, max:95, status:"Unsafe"],
        [min:96, max:100, status:"Very Risky"]
    ])

    evaluateRange("RAM Usage", ram, [
        [min:0, max:50, status:"Safe"],
        [min:51, max:65, status:"Normal"],
        [min:66, max:75, status:"Moderate"],
        [min:76, max:85, status:"Risky"],
        [min:86, max:95, status:"Unsafe"],
        [min:96, max:100, status:"Very Risky"]
    ])

    evaluateRange("Temperature", temp, [
        [min:0, max:50, status:"Safe"],
        [min:51, max:65, status:"Normal"],
        [min:66, max:75, status:"Moderate"],
        [min:76, max:85, status:"Risky"],
        [min:86, max:95, status:"Unsafe"],
        [min:96, max:200, status:"Critical"]
    ])

    evaluateRange("Disk Usage", disk, [
        [min:0, max:50, status:"Safe"],
        [min:51, max:70, status:"Normal"],
        [min:71, max:80, status:"Moderate"],
        [min:81, max:90, status:"Risky"],
        [min:91, max:95, status:"Unsafe"],
        [min:96, max:100, status:"Critical"]
    ])

    evaluateRange("Fan Speed", fan, [
        [min:0, max:2500, status:"Safe"],
        [min:2501, max:3500, status:"Normal"],
        [min:3501, max:4500, status:"Moderate"],
        [min:4501, max:5500, status:"Risky"],
        [min:5501, max:10000, status:"Critical"]
    ])

    // DEVICE INFO
    message.setProperty("Emp_Email", emp_email)

    // FINAL STATUS
    if (alertFlag) {
        message.setProperty("Status", "ALERT")
    } else {
        message.setProperty("Status", "OK")
    }

    return message
}

Step 6: Mail Adapter

After processing, we send the email alert.

Configure the Mail Adapter as shown below:

Step 7: Output (Email Notifications)

Finally, the user receives a structured email:

The email shows:

  • Device details
  • Status (OK or ALERT)
  • Issues table with severity
  • Color-coded results
  • Recommended actions

This makes it very easy for users to quickly understand the problem.

 

This approach shows how SAP CPI can be used not just for integration, but also for building smart monitoring and alerting solutions. 

Thank You, Happy SAP Learning !

entry.title: API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 2: API Governance with Developer Hub
entry.content:
Introduction

In Part 1 of this series, we explored how to build an API-centric integration using the Integration Cell runtime. We activated the required capabilities, created our first API artifact, explored its runtime components, deployed it, and verified its successful deployment using the monitoring console.

After an API is deployed, exposing its runtime endpoint directly to application developers is not sufficient. Enterprise APIs require governance to ensure they are discoverable, documented, secured, versioned, and accessible only to authorized consumers. Without a centralized catalogue, API discovery and onboarding often rely on manual communication, resulting in inconsistent documentation, duplicated effort, and poor governance.

This is where Developer Hub comes into the picture. Developer Hub serves as the centralized catalogue for publishing and discovering APIs, Events, and MCP Servers in SAP Integration Suite. Rather than exposing deployed APIs directly, it enables organizations to bundle one or more APIs into API Products, enrich them with documentation and metadata, and publish them through a single, governed catalogue.

Application developers can browse available API Products, understand their capabilities, subscribe to them by creating developer applications, and obtain the credentials required to securely invoke the published APIs. This approach provides a consistent governance model for managing API discovery, access control, subscriptions, and lifecycle management.

In this blog, you will complete the API lifecycle by creating an API Product, publishing it to Developer Hub, creating and subscribing a developer application, obtaining application credentials, and invoking the secured API endpoint.

By the end of this blog, you will complete the governance and consumption journey shown below:

Discover API → Create API Product → Publish → Create Application → Subscribe → Obtain Credentials → Consume API

Prerequisites

Developer Hub extends API Management by providing a centralized catalogue for publishing and consuming enterprise APIs. The required role collections separate administrative responsibilities from application developer activities, ensuring controlled API governance.

Before proceeding, ensure that:

  • You have completed Part 1 of this series and successfully deployed an API artifact to the Integration Cell runtime.
  • The Developer Hub capability is activated as part of the API Management capability in your SAP Integration Suite tenant.
  • You have the required role collections assigned:
    • AuthGroup.Content.Admin – Required to create, manage, and publish API Products.
    • AuthGroup.API.ApplicationDeveloper – Required to discover published APIs, create developer applications, subscribe to API Products, and obtain application credentials.

With these prerequisites in place, the API governance and consumption journey involves the following steps:

  1. Create an API Product and add the deployed API artifact.
  2. Publish the API Product to Developer Hub.
  3. Create a developer application.
  4. Subscribe the application to the published API Product.
  5. Obtain the generated application credentials.
  6. Invoke the secured API endpoint using the subscribed application's credentials.

This version closely matches the structure of Part 1 while making it clear that Part 2 starts from an already deployed API and focuses on governance and consumption.

Create Your First API Product

With the API artifact successfully deployed in Part 1, the next step is to make it discoverable and consumable through Developer Hub. In enterprise environments, APIs are rarely published individually. Instead, they are grouped into API Products that represent business capabilities such as Procurement, Sales, or Finance. API Products become the unit of publication, subscription, lifecycle management, and access control. An API Product groups one or more related APIs into a single, governed package that application developers can discover, subscribe to, and consume.

In this section, we will create our first API Product by adding the deployed S4Hana Purchase Order API and preparing it for publication.

Open the Content Administration View

Open Developer Hub and navigate to: Admin Center → Content This page provides the administrative interface for managing all publishable assets available in Developer Hub, including APIs, Events, and MCP Servers.

 

Select the Business System

Developer Hub can aggregate APIs from multiple Integration Suite runtimes. Business Systems act as the logical source of published integration assets, allowing organizations to build a unified enterprise API catalog across landscapes. Select the business system that contains the deployed API artifact. Developer Hub retrieves all published integration assets from the selected business system and makes them available for creating products.

Browse Available APIs

Developer Hub lists all deployed API artifacts that are eligible for publication. Before publishing an API, administrators can verify metadata such as version, authentication provider, protocol, and description. This metadata enables consistent governance and helps ensure that only production-ready APIs are exposed to consumers.

After selecting the business system, open the APIs tab.  Each API displays useful metadata such as its name, description, service type, authentication provider, version, and last modified date, making it easy to identify the APIs that should be exposed to application developers. In this example, the S4Hana Purchase Order API that was deployed in Part 1 is available for selection.

Once the required API is selected, the Create Product action becomes available. In the next section, we will create an API Product, configure its metadata, and publish it to Developer Hub for discovery and consumption.

Create and Publish Product

API Products are intended for long-term discovery by application developers. Meaningful names, concise summaries, and descriptive documentation significantly improve discoverability and reduce onboarding effort. Provide the mandatory product details, including the Name and ID. Optionally, add a Short Text and Description to help application developers understand the purpose of the product. The selected APIs are automatically included in the product.

Select Publish to initiate the publication process.

After selecting Publish, Developer Hub confirms that the request has been accepted and displays an information message indicating that the publication request has been created. The actual processing happens asynchronously in the background. You can monitor the progress from Admin Center → Scheduled Requests.

The Scheduled Requests page provides a centralized view of all content creation and update operations within Developer Hub. Publication involves synchronizing metadata across multiple platform services. Processing requests asynchronously improves scalability while allowing administrators to monitor publication progress through Scheduled Requests. Initially, the product publication request appears with the status In Progress, indicating that Developer Hub is validating the product and synchronizing the content.

Once processing completes successfully, the status changes to Success, confirming that the product has been published and is now available in the Developer Hub catalog.

This asynchronous processing model allows Developer Hub to reliably publish products without blocking the user interface, while providing complete visibility into the publication lifecycle

Discover, Subscribe, and Consume the API

At this stage, the API has been successfully designed, deployed, and published. The responsibility now shifts to the Application Developer, whose objective is to discover the published API product, subscribe to it, obtain application credentials, and securely invoke the API.

This self-service experience is one of the key capabilities of Developer Hub. Rather than requesting credentials or endpoint information from the integration team, application developers can independently discover available APIs, subscribe to the required products, and generate credentials needed to access protected APIs.

In this section, we will perform the complete developer journey by:

  1. Discover the published Enterprise Procurement Services product in Developer Hub.
  2. Review the product details and the APIs included in the product.
  3. Subscribe to the product by creating a developer application.
  4. Generate the application credentials (Client ID and Client Secret).
  5. Obtain an OAuth access token using the generated credentials.
  6. Invoke the secured Purchase Order API and validate the response.

By the end of this section, the complete API lifecycle will be demonstrated—from API design and deployment in Integration Suite to secure API consumption by an application developer through Developer Hub.

Browse the Product Catalog

Developer Hub serves as the organization's internal API marketplace. Rather than sharing URLs through emails or documentation, developers search a centralized catalogue to discover available business capabilities. Navigate to the Developer Hub home page. The published Enterprise Procurement Services product is now visible in the catalogue, allowing application developers to discover the APIs exposed by the integration team.

 

Explore the Product

Before integrating an API, developers typically evaluate its purpose, documentation, supported operations, and version. Developer Hub consolidates this information into a single product page, reducing dependency on separate documentation portals. The product page provides an overview of the published asset, including the APIs bundled within the product. In this example, the product contains the S4Hana Purchase order API.

Review the API bundled in the API Product

Interactive API documentation shortens integration time by allowing developers to understand operations, payloads, and responses without switching between multiple documents. Developer Hub automatically renders the OpenAPI specification as interactive API documentation, enabling developers to:

  •   Browse operations
  •   Understand schemas
  •   Download OAS
  •   Generate SDKs
  •   Generate code snippets
  •   Test APIs

This interactive documentation enables developers to understand and integrate with the API without requiring external documentation.

 

Create an Application Subscription

API Products remain inaccessible until an application subscribes to them. Subscriptions establish the trust relationship between an application and the published product while enabling controlled access through OAuth credentials. To consume a protected API, an application must first subscribe to the published API product.

From the product page, select Subscribe → Create New Subscription for Application.

Provide the application details that identify the consumer application.

Subscription

During provisioning, Developer Hub creates the underlying authorization artifacts and generates application credentials. These background operations ensure that applications receive unique identities rather than sharing common credentials.

Immediately after the request is submitted, Developer Hub creates the subscription and begins provisioning the required runtime artifacts, including the application credentials and authorization configuration.
During this period, the application displays a message indicating that the subscription is being finalized. Depending on the runtime and tenant configuration, credential generation may take a short time to complete. Once provisioning finishes successfully, the application subscription becomes active and the generated OAuth credentials are available for use.

 

Every subscribed application receives its own OAuth client. This enables APIs to authenticate individual consuming applications, apply authorization policies, and audit API usage independently. Next, we will retrieve the generated Client ID and Client Secret, obtain an OAuth access token, and invoke the secured Purchase Order API through the Integration Cell endpoint.

 

Invoke the Protected API Using Application Credentials

Developer Hub generates OAuth client credentials. These are standard OAuth 2.0 credentials and can therefore be used with any OAuth-compliant client such as Postman, curl, custom applications, or SDKs. In this blog, Postman is used to demonstrate the end-to-end invocation flow.

 

Invoke the API

Invoke the protected Purchase Order API using the managed endpoint exposed through Integration Cell.

GET /A_PurchaseOrder

The request now contains a valid OAuth bearer token, allowing the Authentication and Authorization policies configured in the API artifact to validate the caller before forwarding the request to the backend S/4HANA system. Upon successful validation, the API returns the requested Purchase Order data.

 

 

Monitor API Execution

Every invocation processed by Integration Cell is recorded as a Message Processing Log (MPL), enabling administrators to monitor API execution and troubleshoot runtime issues. Operational monitoring completes the API lifecycle. Once an API is consumed, administrators need visibility into authentication, authorization, backend connectivity, policy execution, and runtime performance. Integration Cell records every invocation as a Message Processing Log (MPL), enabling troubleshooting without modifying the deployed API.

Navigate to:

Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Monitor Message Processing

The monitoring view displays all API executions along with their processing status, execution duration, correlation identifiers, and runtime metadata. Selecting a message opens detailed execution information for further analysis.

 

 

 

Analyze the API Processing Flow

Opening an individual Message Processing Log provides a graphical representation of the API execution pipeline. The API Model view displays each processing step executed for the request, including:

  • OData2 Sender Adapter
  • Authentication Policy
  • Authorization Policy
  • Request Reply
  • OData Receiver Adapter

For every step, Integration Cell records the execution time, making it easy to identify processing latency or failures within the request pipeline. The accompanying Log Content view provides detailed runtime logs and diagnostics for each execution step, simplifying troubleshooting and operational analysis.

 

Conclusion

In this second part of the series, you completed the governance and consumption phase of the API lifecycle on SAP Integration Suite. Starting with a deployed API artifact, you published it through Developer Hub by creating an API Product, making it discoverable to application developers through a centralized API catalogue.

With this invocation, the complete API lifecycle has been demonstrated—from designing and deploying an API artifact, publishing it through Developer Hub, subscribing to the API Product, obtaining OAuth credentials, securely invoking the endpoint, and finally monitoring its execution in Integration Cell.

entry.title: CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite / The Release Import workflow
entry.content:
The previous post covered how externalized parameters and log levels are stored in Git alongside each integration package, and how the pipeline applies the right values automatically on every deployment. At the end, it noted that the Download → Git → Deploy workflow intentionally stops at TST. This post is about what happens next: how a team takes a controlled, auditable snapshot of everything that has changed since the last release and promotes exactly that delta to production — no more, no less.

  • The Branch Model Revisited
  • Two Workflows, One Controlled Sequence
  • Step 1: Create Release — Cutting the Snapshot
    • What Happens Under the Hood
    • Simulation Mode
  • Step 2: Release Import — Deploying the Delta
    • What You Fill In
    • Analyze Changes: The Delta Calculation
    • Dry Run Before You Commit
    • Execute Deployment
  • What It Does Not Do
  • Key Takeaways

The Branch Model Revisited

The Git branch model was introduced in the Download → Git → Deploy post. It is worth briefly revisiting here, because the Release Import workflow is built directly on top of it.

The repository has two permanent branches.

The development branch is where all active work lands. Every time a developer runs the Download → Git → Deploy workflow, the changes go here. This branch is in constant motion during an active development cycle: packages are downloaded, parameters are updated, new iFlows appear, old ones are removed. It reflects the current state of the DEV tenant in Git.

The main branch represents what has been formally released. Between releases, it is untouched. It holds the state of the repository at the point of the last release — a clean, stable snapshot that every team member can trust as the baseline for production.

The gap between these two branches is the release. When the team is ready to promote, that gap needs to be calculated, reviewed, and deployed. That is exactly what the two release workflows do.

development  ─── [download A] ─── [update params] ─── [download B] ─── HEAD
                                                                           │
                                                                           │  Create Release:
                                                                           │  · merges development → main
                                                                           │  · creates Git tag v1.4.0
                                                                           │  · resets development to main
                                                                           ▼
main         ──────────────────── [v1.3.0] ──────────────────────── [v1.4.0]
                                      │                                    │
                                   base-ref                           target-ref
                                      └──────── Release Import ───────────┘
                                                compare delta, deploy to PRD

Two Workflows, One Controlled Sequence

The release process involves two separate workflows that are run in sequence.

Create Release (git-create-release.yml) is an admin workflow that cuts the snapshot. It merges the development branch into main, creates a Git tag and a GitHub Release, and resets development back to the state of main. The result is a clean, immutable reference point in Git — a tag that will never change and that precisely identifies everything in that release.

Release Import (btp-release-import.yml) is the deployment workflow. It takes two Git references — a base (the previous release tag) and a target (the new release tag) — computes what changed between them, and deploys only those changes to the specified environment. Nothing that did not change is touched.

The separation is deliberate. The snapshot and the deployment are two independent decisions. A team might create the release tag on a Friday and run the import to production on Monday after a final sign-off. The tag is immutable; the deployment can be triggered whenever the team is ready.

Step 1: Create Release — Cutting the Snapshot

The (Admin) GIT Create Release workflow is triggered manually. The inputs are minimal: a release name (which becomes the Git tag, for example v1.4.0) and an optional simulation flag.

  Two inputs: a release name (which becomes the Git tag) and an optional simulation toggle. Everything else is automated.

What Happens Under the Hood

The workflow executes a carefully sequenced set of steps to ensure the release is atomic and recoverable.

Step 1  Check duplicate tag
        └─ if v1.4.0 already exists → stop immediately

Step 2  Rename development → tmp/release-v1.4.0
        └─ takes development out of service during the release cut
        └─ provides recovery point if the run is interrupted
           (restart-safe: re-running the workflow picks up from here)

Step 3  Create pull request: tmp/release-v1.4.0 → main
        └─ programmatic merge preserves full commit history on main
        └─ every download and parameter change from the cycle is auditable

Step 4  Guard against duplicate merge on same commit
        └─ safety check before writing the tag

Step 5  Create Git tag v1.4.0 on main
        └─ publish GitHub Release at that tag
        └─ immutable reference — will never change

Step 6  Recreate development from main at v1.4.0
        └─ next cycle starts from a clean, known baseline

Step 7  Delete tmp/release-v1.4.0
        └─ staging branch cleaned up

A pull request is used for the merge — even though it is merged programmatically — because it preserves the individual commit history from development on main. Every package download, every parameter change, every commit that accumulated during the release cycle is visible in the history of main after the merge. This matters for auditability: the Git history of main tells the full story of how the release was assembled.

Restart safety. If the workflow is interrupted at any point, re-running it with the same release name resumes from where it stopped. The phase detection at the start of the workflow checks whether the release tag exists, whether the staging branch is still present, and whether development needs to be recreated — and picks up accordingly. No manual cleanup is needed if a run fails partway through.

Simulation Mode

Running the workflow with simulation set to true runs a dry-run that stops short of making any permanent change. The workflow creates a pull request from development into main to check mergeability, reports whether the merge would succeed or fail due to conflicts, and then closes the PR without merging. No branch is renamed. No tag is created. No release is published.

This is useful as a pre-check during an active release cycle — a way to verify that development is clean and mergeable before committing to the release.

Step 2: Release Import — Deploying the Delta

With the release tag in place, the team is ready to deploy. The (Admin) BTP Release Manager workflow calculates what changed and, optionally, deploys it.

What You Fill In

The workflow takes four inputs:

  • GIT Base Reference — the starting point of the comparison. For a standard production release this is the previous release tag, for example v1.3.0. For the very first release ever, it would be the initial commit or an early tag that represents the state of production before the pipeline took over.
  • GIT Target Reference — the ending point. This is the new release tag, for example v1.4.0, or the development branch for a TST promotion.
  • Perform deploy — false to calculate the delta and report it without deploying, true to deploy.
  • Target BTP Environment — the environment to deploy to, selected from the configured GitHub Environments.

Four inputs. The base and target refs define the comparison window; perform-deploy controls whether the workflow stops at analysis or proceeds to deployment.

Not only for PRD. The base-ref and target-ref inputs are Git references — not environment names. Nothing in the workflow limits their use to a production promotion. A team can use the Release Import workflow to promote from TST to a pre-production stage, or to redeploy a specific historical tag to any environment. The most common pattern is previous-tag → new-tag for PRD, but the comparison window is fully flexible.

Analyze Changes: The Delta Calculation

Before any deployment decision is made, the workflow runs the Analyze Changes job. This is always executed, regardless of whether perform-deploy is true or false.

The job uses git diff to compare the two references across both the IntegrationPackages and PartnerDirectory directories in the repository. The result is four CSV outputs — the precise deployment manifest for this release:

CSV Scope What it contains

Package uploadIntegrationPackagesPackages added or modified between base-ref and target-ref — will be deployed
Package deleteIntegrationPackagesPackages present in base-ref but absent in target-ref — will be removed from the target environment
PD uploadPartnerDirectoryPartner Directory entries added or modified — will be deployed
PD deletePartnerDirectoryPartner Directory entries present in base-ref but absent in target-ref — will be removed

All four lists are uploaded as a workflow artifact and printed in the run log, so the team can review exactly what will happen before committing to the deployment.

 The four CSV sections printed to the run log. In this example, three packages are queued for upload, one Partner Directory entry for upload, and nothing is being deleted.

This is the answer to the question every release manager asks: what exactly is going to change? The delta calculation answers it precisely, from the Git history, before a single API call is made to BTP.

Dry Run Before You Commit

The recommended pattern for a production release is to run the workflow twice: first with perform-deploy: false to inspect the delta, then with perform-deploy: true to execute it.

Run 1 — Inspect
  perform-deploy: false
  │
  ├─ Analyze Changes (always runs)
  │   └─ produces 4 CSV lists, uploads artifact, prints to log
  └─ Execute Deployment — SKIPPED
      → Review the output: correct packages? Unexpected deletes?

Run 2 — Execute  (same base-ref and target-ref)
  perform-deploy: true
  │
  ├─ Analyze Changes (runs again — always)
  │   └─ re-calculates delta from Git
  └─ Execute Deployment — RUNS
      → Packages deployed, deletions applied, parameters set

Running the analysis twice adds only seconds to the process. The cost is negligible; the confidence it provides is not.

Execute Deployment

When perform-deploy is true, a third job runs after the analysis: Execute Deployment. It receives the four CSV lists from the Analyze Changes job and processes them in order.

The deployment uses the same logic as the Download → Git → Deploy workflow: clean before upload, deploy package artifacts in the order defined by their Rank configuration, apply environment-specific parameter values and log levels after activation. The source for the upload is the target-ref — the new release tag — so the content deployed is precisely what was tagged, not whatever happens to be on a branch at the time of the run.

Deletions are also processed. If a package or Partner Directory entry was present in the base-ref but absent in the target-ref, it is deleted from the target environment. Deletions travel through the pipeline automatically — there is no manual step to remember.

The customer exit hook (cx-derive-iflow-exclusions) is evaluated at deployment time, not at analysis time. This means if a team has a customer exit that dynamically excludes certain iFlows from deployment based on runtime state, those exclusions are calculated fresh on each deploy job execution. Re-running a failed deployment gets a fresh exclusion evaluation, not a stale one from a previous run.

What It Does Not Do

The Release Import workflow does not apply externalized parameters or log levels for packages that were not in the delta. If a package was already deployed to production from the previous release and nothing in that package changed, it is not touched. The parameters and log levels already on the target environment remain as they are. Only the packages that actually changed are processed.

The workflow also does not create or manage the Git tag itself — that is the responsibility of the Create Release workflow. The Release Import workflow is a consumer of tags, not a producer. This keeps the two concerns cleanly separated: snapshot management is one action, deployment is another.

Key Takeaways

  • The release process is two workflows in sequence: Create Release cuts an immutable snapshot (merges development → main, creates a Git tag, resets development); Release Import computes and deploys the delta between two tags.
  • The snapshot and the deployment are independent decisions. The tag is created once and is immutable; the deployment can be triggered at any time after that, against any target environment.
  • The delta calculation is always explicit and inspectable. The four CSV lists (packages to upload, packages to delete, Partner Directory entries to upload, Partner Directory entries to delete) are available as a workflow artifact before any deployment takes place.
  • The recommended pattern is two runs: first with perform-deploy: false to review the delta, then with perform-deploy: true to execute. The second run re-calculates the delta before deploying.
  • Deletions are automatic. A package or PartnerID removed from the repository between the base-ref and the target-ref will be deleted from the target environment without a separate manual action.
  • The Create Release workflow is restart-safe. If interrupted, re-triggering with the same release name resumes from the correct phase without manual cleanup.
  • The base and target refs are not limited to tags. Any Git ref can be used — a branch name,  or a tag. The most common pattern for production is previous-release-tag → new-release-tag, but the comparison window is flexible.

The next post in this series covers Access Policies as code: how governance rules are version-controlled, promoted through the pipeline, and applied consistently across environments — following the same Git-first pattern as integration packages.

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

Series overview: The Challenge: Large Platform, Demanding Operations

entry.title: API-Centric Integration in SAP Integration Suite: A new paradigm for API & Integration practitioner
entry.content:
This blog is co-authored with @artigopalan (PM Director, SAP Integration Suite)

Introduction

Working with SAP Integration Suite, you know the depth it brings across its two most valued capabilities: Cloud Integration (CPI), the engine behind message mediation, transformation, and process orchestration, and API Management, where APIs are governed, secured, protected, and published. Together, they power and integrate some of the most complex enterprise landscapes in the world. And yet, as mature and capable as each is in its own right, there's a natural question that practitioners regularly ask: what if these two capabilities truly converged? What if the design experience, the runtime, and the observability layer spoke a single language, one that felt native to both the integration developer and the API architect? That question is what API-Centric Integration is designed to answer.

One Coin. Two Sides. One Experience.

Practitioners have long known that API management and integration are not independent concerns. Every API is an integration surface. Every integration exposes a contract that could benefit from API-level governance. The separation has been more a consequence of how solutions and their technical stacks evolved than a reflection of how the work actually flows.

API-Centric Integration is built on a simple but powerful premise: the design language, the runtime, and the observability plane should be unified, empowered by AI. Not loosely coupled. Not bridged after the fact. Unified by design.

What This Means in Practice

A common design language

Customers can now define, secure, and manage APIs using a single, consistent authoring experience that natively supports mediation and augmentation steps. The modeling itself occurs on a newly introduced artifact type simply called the API, and this, too, has been consciously kept distinct. It is not the same as an IFlow in Cloud Integration, and it is not the same as the API proxy or API artifact you may know from the classic API Management functionality. It is a first-principles rethink of what an API artifact should look like when integration capability is native to it, not layered on top.

If you are a Cloud Integration practitioner, the concepts inside it will feel familiar: routing, transformation, enrichment, logging. If you are an API architect, the governance constructs like policies, rate limiting, and security schemes are first-class citizens. The two disciplines that have long lived on separate stacks, with separate tools and separate mental models, now meet inside a single artifact.

Underneath this experience runs a single, SAP-managed cloud-native runtime called the Integration Cell. It exists to make the unified design experience above possible, with the deployment, scaling, and execution details handled for you.

 

Agent-readiness, with MCP Gateway

This is perhaps the most forward-looking aspect of API-Centric Integration. APIs built within this new model can be elevated to MCP Servers, the protocol standard for exposing tools and data to AI agents and large language models, without additional development effort. It is a deliberate architectural choice to ensure that the APIs you build today are natively consumable by the agentic applications and Joule-powered workflows of tomorrow. The API you publish for a human consumer and the tools you expose to an AI agent are, again, two sides of the same coin. 

Follow this post to learn more about the MCP Gateway.

 

A single observability pane

One of the most persistent operational challenges in running both stacks has been complexity: separate monitoring dashboards, separate log pipelines, and no single place to understand what is actually happening across your API and integration landscape.

API-Centric Integration changes that. It runs on a single execution engine with a unified pipeline for logs and traces. When something needs attention, you look in one place.

But observability in API-Centric Integration goes further than just consolidating logs. The same unified pane brings in analytics capabilities that were previously either absent or fragmented. Usage patterns, traffic trends, and performance metrics across your APIs and MCP servers are visible together, in a single dashboard, broken down by the dimensions that actually matter: by API, by consumer, by agent, by tool.

For teams that need to stay ahead of problems rather than just react to them, the analytics layer includes AI-based anomaly detection that learns the normal traffic behaviour of your APIs and flags deviations before they become incidents. Alongside that, traffic predictions give you a forward-looking view of expected API call volumes, so capacity planning and resource decisions are grounded in data rather than guesswork.

The result is an observability experience that is not just unified, but genuinely intelligent. One place to look, whether you are investigating a live issue, reviewing consumption trends, or planning for what comes next.

A simplified commercial model

For integrations built with this new design, there is no double-counting across the API and integration layers. You are running one thing. You pay for one thing.

A foundation for AI Gateway capabilities

Beyond MCP, a rich AI Gateway will be integrated into this design experience over time, bringing model routing, token governance, and LLM observability into the same unified surface. The integration layer is becoming the AI governance layer. API-Centric Integration is where that convergence begins.

What this is, and what it isn't

API-Centric Integration is relevant for greenfield projects: new APIs, new integration flows, new architectures built on a clean slate. Existing APIs managed through the current API Management capability and existing CPI iFlows continue to operate exactly as they are.

Think of it as the recommended starting point for net-new work, the architecture you would choose if you were starting today, knowing what you now know about where integration and AI are heading.

One additional note on scope: the Integration Cell is a new runtime introduced specifically for this paradigm. It sits alongside, not on top of, your existing Cloud Integration runtime and Edge Integration Cell. These are not competing runtimes. They are the right tool for the right job, depending on where you are in your journey.

The bigger picture

SAP Integration Suite has always been the connective tissue of enterprise landscapes. What is changing is the nature of what needs to be connected and what needs to consume those connections. Business processes increasingly involve AI agents that require governed, observable, low-latency access to enterprise data and functions. In that world, the distinction between an integration runtime and an API runtime becomes not just unnecessary, but actively counterproductive.

API-Centric Integration is our answer to that shift: a unified foundation that respects the depth of what practitioners have built, while opening the door to a design paradigm that is ready for the agent-first era.

Getting started

API-Centric Integration is available now as part of SAP Integration Suite. Full product documentation, including a guided walkthrough of the API Artifact, policy modelling, and Integration Cell setup, is available on the SAP Help Portal:

API-Centric Integration - SAP Help Portal 

Rollout status

The rollout is actively in progress across SAP's managed landscape:

  • Trial landscape: Already updated and available for exploration today.
  • Stage 1 data centers: Already updated and available for exploration today.
  • Stage 2 data centers: Currently receiving software updates and feature enablement.

For the full rollout schedule and data center details, refer to SAP Note 3418154 .

If you are an existing SAP Integration Suite customer, no action is required on your part to receive the update. When the rollout reaches your data center, the capability will be available in your tenant. Your existing integration flows and API proxies are unaffected.

We will be sharing more on hands-on guidance, and product deep-dives in the coming weeks. Questions and feedback are welcome in the SAP Community comments below.

entry.title: Built an MCP Server for SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

I'm Jayesh Sinha, an SAP Integration Suite Architect with over 11 years of experience designing and implementing integration solutions across SAP and non-SAP landscapes. Throughout my career, I have worked extensively with SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), helping organizations build scalable, reliable, and maintainable integration flows. This hands-on experience with CPI's monitoring, troubleshooting, and operational challenges is what inspired me to explore how AI agents and the Model Context Protocol can simplify day-to-day integration work. This blog reflects my journey of combining enterprise integration expertise with modern AI tooling to make CPI operations more conversational and efficient.

SAP Cloud Integration, also known as SAP CI, is widely used for designing, deploying, and monitoring integration flows between SAP and non-SAP systems. While CPI provides strong monitoring and troubleshooting capabilities, checking message logs, failed messages, runtime artifacts, and integration packages usually requires manual navigation inside the SAP Integration Suite UI.

In this blog, we will build a Python-based MCP server that connects to SAP CI and exposes CI operations as tools. Then, we will connect an AI agent to this MCP server so the agent can inspect CI logs, identify failed messages, summarize issues, and help with troubleshooting.

Instead of manually opening CPI, filtering logs, and reading technical payloads, the AI agent can call predefined CPI tools through the MCP server and return a clear summary.

What Is MCP?

MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a standard way for AI applications and agents to connect with external tools, systems, APIs, files, and data sources.

In simple terms, MCP allows an AI agent to use tools safely and consistently.

For example, instead of giving an AI model direct access to SAP CPI credentials and asking it to figure out API calls, we create an MCP server that exposes controlled tools

High-Level Architecture

User Queation -> AI Agent-> MCP Client -> Python MCP Server -> SAP CPI OData APIs -> CPI Logs, Artifacts, Packages, Metadata

The MCP server is responsible for:

Authenticating with SAP CPI
Calling CPI OData APIs
Handling JSON responses
Exposing CPI actions as MCP tools
Keeping credentials outside the AI prompt
Providing a controlled access layer

Benefits of This Approach

This solution provides several benefits.

It makes CPI troubleshooting more conversational. Developers can ask questions instead of manually navigating logs.

It separates AI reasoning from enterprise system access. The AI agent does not directly handle CPI credentials.

It creates a reusable integration layer. The same MCP server can be used by different MCP-compatible clients.

It improves maintainability. CPI API logic is centralized in one server.

It is extensible. More CPI tools can be added later as needed.

It supports safer automation. Read-only tools can be introduced first, while write operations can be added later with stronger controls.

Configuration

you can use CHATGPT/CLAUDE Desktop to connect to the MCP server.

1) Download MCP server in you local system from below GIT repository.

https://github.com/jacksnh/CPI-MCP-SERVER 

2) Modify "claude_desktop_config.json" to add the MCP server

"ci-mcp-server2": {
    "command": "python",
    "args": [
        "C:\\Users\\Username\\path\\to\\your\\server.py"
    ]
}

3) Restart Claude desktop and you will see below Server in Developer Section

4) Now your AI agent is ready to interact with MCP server

 

Conclusion

Integrating SAP Cloud Integration with an AI agent through an MCP server represents a meaningful step forward in how developers and integration teams interact with enterprise middleware. Rather than manually sifting through logs and navigating the SAP Integration Suite UI, teams can now ask plain-language questions and receive clear, actionable answers in seconds.

entry.title: MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite: Your APIs, Ready for the Age of Agents
entry.content:
This blog is co-authored with @artigopalan  (PM Director, SAP Integration Suite)

This post is a follow-up to our earlier introduction of API-Centric Integration. If you haven't read that yet, we recommend starting there.

Introduction

The way enterprise software gets used is changing. AI agents, autonomous programs that reason, plan, and take action, are no longer a future concept. They are arriving in production environments now, and they need to access your enterprise systems, data, and processes to be useful.

The question for every enterprise architect and IT leader is the same: how do you let AI agents into your systems without losing the governance and control you have spent years building?

In our previous post, we introduced API-Centric Integration: a unified design and runtime experience that brings API governance and integration mediation together in a single artifact. We closed with a forward-looking note on agent-readiness. This post is where that story continues.

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is the answer.

A quick glossary before we begin

MCP is still a relatively new term in most enterprise conversations, so it is worth grounding the three concepts that matter here before going further.

An MCP Tool is a named, callable entity that an AI agent can discover and invoke. Think of it as a function with a description: the agent reads that description, decides whether the tool is relevant to the task at hand, and calls it. A tool might represent "Get Customer Order Status," "Reconcile Inventory Levels Across Distribution Centers," or "Trigger a Purchase Order Approval." Tools are the atomic unit of agent-accessible functionality.

An MCP Server is the runtime host that exposes one or more tools over the Model Context Protocol, an open standard that any MCP-compatible agent or framework can connect to. If tools are the endpoints, the MCP Server is the API server hosting them. It makes tools discoverable and invokable in a standardized way, decoupled from how the underlying system actually works.

An MCP Gateway is the enterprise control plane for MCP. Where an MCP Server hosts tools, the Gateway governs access to them: enforcing authentication, authorization, rate limiting, and payload protection, and providing full monitoring and traceability. SAP Integration Suite's MCP Gateway aggregates tools from SAP APIs, non-SAP APIs, integration flows, data sources, and external MCP Servers into a single governed entry point.

The problem with the way things work today - and why your existing investment is the solution

Most enterprises that have invested in SAP Integration Suite already have a curated, governed library of APIs: connecting SAP S/4HANA, Business AI Platform, third-party systems, and custom backends. That represents years of work: designing integrations, securing them, documenting them, and operationalizing them.

The challenge until now has been that none of that investment was directly accessible to AI agents. Agents do not call REST APIs natively the way developers do. They need tools: described, discoverable, and callable via a standardized protocol.
