Without that, every agent project forces the same painful workaround: a developer manually reads the backend schema and translates it into the agent's tool-calling code, field names, types, nested structures, all hardcoded. That mapping is brittle. If the backend schema changes, nothing tells the agent. The integration breaks, silently. The next project repeats the same work from zero. And security and governance are only as consistent as each developer's diligence.

The agent can reason. It still cannot reach your systems without someone hand-building the bridge, every time.

MCP Gateway closes that gap without asking you to rebuild anything. Your curated APIs become agent-ready tools. Your existing governance models: RBAC, rate limits, and monitoring carry forward into the agentic layer. The work you have done is not a legacy to migrate away from. It is the platform your AI strategy runs on.

Introducing MCP Gateway

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is organized around three things:

Connect. Turn APIs and services into discoverable, reusable tools for agent use. API specifications are automatically translated into MCP schema and augmented with natural language semantics, so agents understand not just the structure of a tool but what it is for. No manual schema translation. No hardcoding. Backend connectivity follows familiar conventions: API Providers and BTP Destinations are used to discover and connect to SAP and non-SAP backend systems, so the connectivity model your teams already rely on carries forward into the agentic layer. Developer Hub extends naturally from an API catalog to a tool and MCP catalog: developers and agent builders can discover available MCP tools, understand their purpose and schema, and subscribe, using the same experience they already use for APIs

Govern. Apply policies, authentication, and usage controls for agents to access tools and systems. Rate limiting, IP blocking, authentication based on OIDC from SAP  (XSUAA) and third-party identity providers, and payload protection all apply to agent traffic with the same rigor as human-facing API traffic. Agents only see and can invoke the tools they are explicitly authorised for: essential in multi-agent environments where different agents have different roles and should have different access boundaries.

Analyze. Provide visibility and traceability across agents, tools, and enterprise landscapes. Every tool invocation by every agent is logged with full traceability, supporting compliance requirements, operational governance, and usage analytics. You have the same visibility into agent behavior that you have today into the transactions handled by SAP Integration Suite, including comprehensive logs, traces, and intelligent analytics.

 

MCP Gateway as the endorsed pathway for SAP API access

This is not a standalone capability sitting outside SAP's broader architecture thinking. SAP's Architecture Center reference architecture for A2A and MCP for Interoperability defines how enterprise AI agent ecosystems should be structured, and SAP Integration Suite's MCP Gateway sits at its core.

In practical terms, if you are deploying AI agents that interact with SAP systems, MCP Gateway is not just a convenient option. It is SAP's architecturally and policy-endorsed approach to doing so in a compliant, governed, and auditable way.

The recommendation is clear: external third-party agents should use MCP Gateway to access SAP systems. Customer-managed pro-code and low-code agents deployed in the SAP BTP context should use MCP Gateway to access SAP systems. Third-party MCP Servers should federate with the MCP Gateway to access SAP systems.

An important distinction: MCP is a connectivity protocol, not a semantic layer

The Architecture Center makes a point here that is easy to overlook and consequential if you do.

MCP standardizes how agents call tools and APIs. It does not add business context or improve the accuracy of AI results. Connecting an MCP server directly to raw SAP transactional APIs without semantic enrichment leads to poor entity discovery accuracy, excessive token consumption, and, for write operations, a real risk of incorrect business transactions. An agent that calls a raw OData endpoint without understanding what the fields mean, or without the business context to distinguish a purchase order from a blanket agreement, will reason poorly and act incorrectly.

This is why the design of an MCP tool matters as much as the platform that hosts it. MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite handles the governed, enterprise-grade concerns: authentication, authorization, rate limiting, payload protection, and observability across SAP and non-SAP APIs. What it does not do, and is not designed to do -> is to decide how to shape your tools.

An API Artifact that exposes nine atomic CRUD endpoints as nine separate tools will produce an MCP Server that forces an agent to invent the orchestration logic a good tool designer should have encoded. The right instinct is to design tools around business outcomes rather than API operations: find_at_risk_suppliers_for_material rather than GET /assmnt/PlantMaterialRisk/{plantId}. The platform enables either. The design is yours.

Two typical use cases

The following scenarios show what becomes possible when your existing SAP investment meets the agentic layer.

Use Case 1: Third-Party AI Platform Accessing SAP Business Data

A retailer uses Microsoft Copilot Studio to build procurement agents for their buying team. The agents need to query SAP product catalogs, check supplier agreements, and initiate purchase requisitions. Historically, this required a custom integration project where developers hand-translated backend schemas into tool-calling code.

With the MCP Gateway, SAP's procurement APIs are exposed as MCP tools through a hosted MCP Server. Copilot Studio agents connect via the standard MCP protocol, authenticate via OIDC, and invoke only the tools they are authorized for. The IT team governs access from a single control plane in Integration Suite, fully aligned with the endorsed architecture from SAP's Architecture Center.

Use Case 2: Multi-Agent HR Process Automation on SAP BTP

An enterprise deploys multiple specialized agents on SAP BTP for HR: one for onboarding, one for payroll queries, one for performance data. Each is built differently: the onboarding agent is low-code via Joule Studio (fka SAP Build), the payroll agent is pro-code using SAP Cloud SDK for AI.

Both agents access SAP SuccessFactors and third-party HR data sources via the MCP Gateway. Tool authorization is scoped per agent role: the onboarding agent cannot access payroll tools, and vice versa. The architecture follows SAP's golden path for agentic AI interoperability.

What it shields you from

MCP is still evolving rapidly. The specification has already undergone significant architectural changes in 2026. Running self-managed MCP servers means tracking that specification roadmap closely, managing upgrade cycles, and absorbing the security implications of each revision. Both patterns of self-managed MCP servers, whether hosted externally or on SAP BTP, place the full operational responsibility for platform stability, security hardening, credential lifecycle, and spec compatibility squarely on the customer or partner deploying them.

For a detailed breakdown of the cross-cutting concerns every MCP operator must address, including authentication, token exchange, rate limiting, observability, and lifecycle management, the Third-Party MCP Access reference architecture on SAP's Architecture Center is the definitive starting point.

The bigger picture

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is the natural evolution of the API Gateway into the agentic era. The core promise is the same: bring your systems together under governed, observable, secure access. The difference is that the consumer is no longer just a developer or an application. It is an AI agent.

SAP Integration Suite has always been the connective tissue of enterprise landscapes. MCP Gateway is what makes that connective tissue agent-aware. The APIs you have already built, the integration flows already running, the S/4HANA function modules already deployed: all of it can now be made consumable by agents through a single governed surface, with the same authentication, observability, and policy enforcement you apply to human-facing traffic today.

Getting started

MCP Gateway is available now as part of SAP Integration Suite Premium and Enhanced Editions. Full documentation is available on the SAP Help Portal. You can also get started by trying it out using the SAP BTP trial or the Free Service Plan.

We will be sharing deep dives and hands-on tutorials in the coming weeks. Questions and feedback are welcome in the SAP Community comments below.

entry.title: Turn SAP APIs into MCP Tools for AI Agents using SAP Integration Suite Free Trial
entry.content:
The MCP Capability for SAP Integration Suite is finally here, and everyone can try it now!

This last week I've been playing around with the new MCP Server artifact in SAP Integration Suite and created an end-to-end use case that I think is worth sharing with the community.

The idea is simple: take an official SAP API from the Business Accelerator Hub (in this example we will use the Business Partner (A2X) API for S/4HANA Public Cloud, as it has a public sandbox that we can use without the need to have a S/4HANA system), expose it through Integration Suite as an MCP server, and connect it to any MCP-compatible AI client so you can query business data using natural language.  All of this on a free trial account (you can get one here).

Before we dive deep into development I recommend checking this blog to get more details on what is a MCP Server, MCP Tool, and MCP Gateway and how they will work together in SAP Integration Suite to become the endorsed pathway for SAP API access: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/mcp-gateway-in-sap-integration-suite-your-apis-ready-for-the-age-of-agents/ba-p/14438250

What you need before we start:

  • A BTP trial account (free at https://www.sap.com/products/technology-platform/trial.html)
  • Create a subaccount (the MCP capability is available in both USA and Singapore regions)
  • Create an instance of SAP Integration Suite with the trial plan
  • Activate the Cloud Integration and API Management capabilities in SAP IS (and assign the required roles to your user in the BTP Cockpit)
  • Enable the new Integration Cell runtime (go to Settings -> Runtimes)
  • A SAP Process Integration Runtime service instance with plan integration-flow created in your Cloud Foundry space, with a service key. This gives you the OAuth credentials (clientid, clientsecret, tokenurl) needed to call the MCP server endpoint.
  • Any MCP-compatible AI client (Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, Continue, or any agent framework supporting MCP)
  • The Business Partner API spec JSON downloaded from SAP Business Accelerator Hub, plus your API key from there

The architecture


The AI client connects to the MCP Server over streamable HTTP, authenticating with an OAuth Bearer token from SAP BTP.
The MCP Server exposes the Business Partner OData operations as tools that the AI model can discover and call.
When a tool is invoked, the MCP Server forwards the request to the API Artifact, which acts as a proxy to the SAP sandbox.
A Content Modifier policy on the API Artifact injects the Business Accelerator Hub API key before hitting the backend — so the AI client only ever needs the OAuth token, no backend credentials.

Note: The "Trust Upstream MCP Authorization" setting on the API Artifact is what makes the internal call work — without it, the API Artifact will reject the request from the MCP Server as unauthorized.

Step 1: Create the API Artifact

Go to your Package → Add → API.
Select Integration Cell as the Runtime profile
Choose URL or Specification as the method
Select Upload and then:

  • upload the Business Partner JSON spec downloaded from the Business Accelerator Hub
  • set the URL to https://sandbox.api.sap.com
  • service type ODATA
  • base path /business-partner-api

Click on Add and Open in API Designer.

In the Policies tab, add a Content Modifier after the Authorization policy. Under Message Header, add:

  • Action: Create
  • Name: APIKey
  • Source Type: Constant
  • Source Value: <your-hub.sap.com-API-key>

In the Authorization policy, check "Trust Upstream MCP Authorization" — this tells the API artifact not to re-validate auth when the MCP server calls it internally. Set the Scope to ESBMessaging.send.

In Target Endpoint, set the full backend URL: https://sandbox.api.sap.com/s4hanacloud/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER

Deploy.

Step 2: Create the MCP Server

Go back to your Package and click on Add → MCP Server → Source Type: API (from a deployed API artifact on Integration Cell):

  • Pick your BusinessPartner API artifact
  • Give it a Name
  • Set MCP Path to /business-partner

Then on the Tools step select the operations you want — select just the GET /A_BusinessPartner if you want to keep it simple. You will be able to add more tools after.

In the Authorization policy, change the scope to match what the integration-flow service key produces: ESBMessaging.send

Deploy.

Step 3: Get credentials

In BTP Cockpit → Cloud Foundry → your space → Service Instances → find the SAP Process Integration Runtime instance with plan integration-flow → create a Service Key. You'll get a clientid, clientsecret and tokenurl.

Fetch a token:

curl -X POST "<tokenurl>/oauth/token" \
  -u "<clientid>:<clientsecret>" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials"

Step 4: Connect your AI client

You can find you MCP server endpoint in the monitoring area of SAP Integration Suite or in the general information of your MCP Server artifact:

https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/business-partner

The transport is streamable HTTP (not SSE). Pass the OAuth token as a Bearer header in the Authorization field of your MCP client configuration. The exact way to do this depends on your client — most have an MCP server settings file where you can set the URL, transport type, and custom headers.

Once it's working, you can ask your AI assistant things like "list the top 5 business partners" and it calls the MCP tool, hits the SAP sandbox, and returns relevant mock data.

I hope this use case can be a solid starting point for building MCP servers on SAP Integration Suite. You can use this capability to expose any API, from SAP or non-SAP.
I will be covering more advanced use cases in the next series of this post. Stay tuned!

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks

Part 3 of the SAP Event Mesh beginner series.

A quick recap

By the end of Part 2, you had events publishing into Event Mesh over HTTP and CPI consuming them from a queue.

Part 1 (this post):  EDA concepts + provisioning the Event Mesh instance, queues, subscriptions, and service key (https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-1-event-driven-architecture-topics-queues/ba-p/14428493)

Part 2: Testing with Postman + CPI iFlow configuration (publish & consume) (https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-2-testing-with-postman-amp-cpi-iflow/ba-p/14428855)

Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks 
Part 4:  Common errors, fixes, and a quick-reference cheat sheet

But there's a catch with everything we've done so far. Every consumption approach in Part 2 was pull-based — your CPI iFlow (AMQP Sender, or the REST /consumption call) reaches out to the queue and asks "any messages for me?" That works, but it means CPI is doing the polling, and there's always a small delay.

There's a second delivery model that flips this around: the webhook. Instead of CPI pulling, Event Mesh pushes — the moment a message lands in the queue, Event Mesh calls your endpoint. No polling, near real-time, and it's the pattern most S/4HANA → CPI integrations actually use in the wild.

If you went looking for "webhook" on the Queue screen and couldn't find it — you're not missing anything. It lives somewhere else, and we'll get there.

 

1. Pull vs Push — when to use which

Both models start the same way: a queue subscribed to your topics collects messages. The difference is who initiates delivery.

  Pull (Part 2) Push (this post)
Who initiatesCPI reaches out to the queueEvent Mesh calls your endpoint
CPI adapterAMQP Sender / REST consumptionHTTPS Sender (receives the call)
TimingPolling intervalNear real-time
Best forBatch-style / CPI-driven pacingEvent-driven, low-latency S/4HANA → CPI

A webhook subscription connects your webhook to a queue, and when messages reach that queue, it delivers them to your webhook in near real time. Because there's no polling, it consumes fewer resources on both sides.

For most "react to an S/4HANA business event" scenarios, push (webhook) is the right choice.

2. Build the receiving iFlow first

A webhook needs somewhere to push to — an HTTPS endpoint that's ready and waiting. In CPI, that's an iFlow with an HTTP Sender adapter. Its address becomes your webhook URL.

  1. Create a new iFlow (e.g. EventMesh_Webhook_Receiver).
  2. On the Sender → HTTPS adapter:
    • Address: something like /eventmesh-webhook → the full URL becomes https://<your-cpi-tenant>/http/eventmesh-webhook
    • Authorization: User Role, role ESBMessaging.send (this is what the webhook's OAuth token will need permission for).
  3. Add whatever processing you need after the sender (map the CloudEvent, call an OData API, etc.).
  4. Deploy the iFlow — the endpoint must be live before you create the webhook, or the handshake (next section) will fail.

 

✅Tip: Copy the deployed endpoint URL from Monitor → Manage Integration Content → your iFlow → Endpoints. That exact URL is what you'll paste as the webhook URL.

3. Get the credential that lets the webhook call into CPI

Here's the single most important thing to understand about webhooks, and where almost everyone gets confused:

⚠️The webhook authenticates INTO CPI — so it uses CPI's inbound credential, not the Event Mesh service key.

Everything up to now used the Event Mesh key (so CPI could talk to Event Mesh). A webhook is the opposite direction: Event Mesh is now the caller, and it needs credentials to get into your CPI endpoint.

Those credentials come from a Process Integration Runtime service instance:

  1. In your CPI subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → Create → service Process Integration Runtime, plan integration-flow.
  2. Add the role/grant for ESBMessaging.send (API access).
  3. Create a Service Key on it → note the clientid, clientsecret, and tokenurl.

These three values are what secure the webhook. Keep them separate in your head from the Event Mesh key — mixing them up is the #1 webhook failure.

4. Create the webhook

Now the part you couldn't find. It's not on the Queue screen — it's on the message client.

  1. In Event Mesh, open the Overview tab and click your Message Client → this opens the Webhooksconfiguration screen → click Create.
  2. Fill in:
Field Value
Namewebhook-3pl (any name)
Queue NameS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3pl (the queue to attach to)
Webhook URLhttps://<your-cpi-tenant>/http/eventmesh-webhook (the deployed HTTPS Sender endpoint)
Quality of Service1 (recommended — see section 6)
AuthenticationOAuth2 Client Credentials
Client ID / Secret / Token URLfrom the Process Integration Runtime service key (section 3)
  1. Save. On success you'll see the subscription added.

 

From here, any message that lands in the 3pl queue is pushed to your iFlow — no polling, no AMQP Sender needed.

5. The handshake (what it is, why it fails)

Before Event Mesh sends a single real message, it does a handshake to make sure your endpoint actually agreed to receive pushes.

The handshake is an OPTIONS call to your webhook URL carrying a WebHook-Request-Origin header. Your endpoint must reply with a WebHook-Allowed-Origin header whose value matches that origin (or is *). Only after a successful handshake does Event Mesh start publishing messages to the webhook.

The good news: a properly deployed CPI HTTPS Sender endpoint handles this automatically. The usual reasons a handshake fails:

  • The iFlow isn't deployed or the endpoint isn't reachable.
  • The credentials are wrong (remember: CPI runtime key, not Event Mesh key).
  • The endpoint requires an auth the webhook isn't sending.

If the webhook shows as not active or "handshake failed," check those three before anything else.

6. QoS and the retry behavior

When you create the webhook you pick a Quality of Service:

  • QoS 0 (at most once): Event Mesh posts the message to your webhook and deletes it from the queue regardlessof whether your endpoint succeeded. Fast, but you can lose messages if CPI was down.
  • QoS 1 (at least once): Event Mesh posts the message and waits for a 2XX response as acknowledgment. Any 2XX = success, message removed. Anything else = the message stays and gets retried.

That retry behavior is worth knowing: on a non-2XX response the service resends roughly every 15 minutes, gradually slowing the frequency, and keeps redelivering for up to 30 days unless your webhook positively acknowledges. So a broken downstream won't silently drop events — but it also means a misbehaving endpoint can pile up retries.

✅Tip: For anything you can't afford to lose (master data, business events), use QoS 1. You can pause a webhook during maintenance and resume it later without losing queued messages.

7. Troubleshooting

Symptom Likely cause Fix
Handshake failed / webhook not activeiFlow not deployed, or endpoint unreachableDeploy the iFlow; confirm the endpoint URL from Monitor → Endpoints
401 on deliveryWrong credential direction — used the Event Mesh keyUse the Process Integration Runtime client id/secret/token URL
403 on deliveryOAuth client lacks the API roleGrant ESBMessaging.send to the CPI runtime instance
Messages pile up in the queue, never deliveredEndpoint returns non-2XX (QoS 1 keeps retrying)Fix the receiving iFlow so it returns 2XX; check CPI monitoring
Nothing arrives at allQueue isn't subscribed to the right topic (case-sensitive!)Re-check the queue's topic subscription — same gotcha as the rest of the series

8. Quick Reference

🔒Replace all placeholders with your own values. Never publish real secrets.

Item Value
Where to createEvent Mesh → Overview → Message Client → Webhooks → Create
Webhook URLhttps://<your-cpi-tenant>/http/<iflow-address> (deployed HTTPS Sender endpoint)
Auth typeOAuth2 Client Credentials
Auth sourceProcess Integration Runtime service key (NOT the Event Mesh key)
Required CPI roleESBMessaging.send
HandshakeOPTIONS + WebHook-Request-Origin → reply WebHook-Allowed-Origin (match or *)
QoS 0delete after post, regardless of result
QoS 1needs 2XX ack; retries ~15 min, backs off, up to 30 days
Maintenancepause / resume the webhook

What's next

Webhooks complete the delivery picture: Part 2 gave you the pull model (CPI reaches out), and this post gives you the push model (Event Mesh reaches in). For real-time S/4HANA → CPI event handling, push is usually what you want.

The one idea to carry away: credential direction. Publishing/consuming uses the Event Mesh key; the webhook uses your CPI runtime key, because the caller and callee have swapped.

In Part 4 — Troubleshooting & Quick Reference, we'll bring the whole series together: every error you're likely to hit across setup, publishing, consuming, and webhooks — with causes, fixes, and a one-page cheat sheet to keep open while you build.

🔒Security note: All client IDs, secrets, tenant URLs, and endpoints in this post are placeholders. Use your own values, keep them out of public posts, and rotate anything that's been exposed.

Primary Tag: SAP Integration Suite Tags: SAP Event Mesh, Cloud Integration, SAP CPI, Webhooks, OAuth2, Event-Driven Architecture, SAP BTP

entry.title: Designing JMS Queues for Scale in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Designing JMS Queues for Scale in SAP Integration Suite

The most difficult integration bottlenecks are the ones that remain invisible during the testing phase, and surfaces when production volumes increase.

In SAP Integration Suite, this scenario usually plays out around JMS queues. While JMS queues are the backbone of resilient, decoupled architectures, they run on a shared resource pool within your tenant, managed by SAP. Understanding these boundaries early during the design phase can turn production deployments from a guessing game into a predictable outcome.

In real implementation projects, JMS queue limitations usually become visible only when message volumes increase or downstream systems slow down. This article summarizes the design considerations I have found most important when my team is building and supporting JMS-based integrations in SAP Integration Suite.

 

SAP JMS - Core Capabilities

The JMS adapter in SAP Cloud Integration is the primary mechanism for asynchronous, event-driven integration patterns because it provides four core capabilities:

  • Asynchronous Decoupling: Senders drop payloads into the queue and immediately disconnect, protecting the source application from downstream performance bottlenecks.
  • Guaranteed Delivery: Messages are persisted in the queue until the consuming integration flow (iFlow) explicitly acknowledges successful processing.
  • Native Redelivery: Any processing rollback caused by an unhandled exception results in message redelivery according to the configured retry interval and exponential backoff settings.
  • First-In, First-Out (FIFO) Processing: Messages within a specific queue are processed sequentially in the exact order they were received. However, when multiple concurrent consumers are enabled using Non-Exclusive Access, messages are processed in parallel and completion order is no longer guaranteed.

Understanding the Resource Limits

A common architectural misconception is that JMS resource exhaustion is driven purely by open socket connections or message storage volume. In reality, SAP Cloud Integration enforces tenant-level quotas for JMS resources such as queues, concurrent consumers, concurrent transactions, and message storage. As more queues and consumers are deployed, these shared tenant limits are consumed, making efficient queue design essential for scalability.

Depending on tenant licensing and SAP provisioning, the JMS broker resource ceiling can reach the following maximum thresholds:

Individual JMS Queues: Default starts at 30 and scales up to 100 maximum.

Storage Capacity: Default starts at 9.3 GB and scales up to 30 GB maximum.

Concurrent JMS Transactions: Default starts at 150 and scales up to 500 maximum.

Concurrent Consumers: Default starts at 150 and scales up to 500 maximum.

The maximum number of consumers for one JMS Sender iFlow is calculated as:

Consumers = No of Runtime Nodes × No of Concurrent Processes

Runtime nodes are also referred to worker nodes.

 

Mathematical Sizing Scenarios

The impact of the Number of Concurrent Processes setting becomes much clearer when viewed against the maximum limit of 500 concurrent JMS consumers available in a tenant. The following examples illustrate how different runtime configurations influence resource consumption.

1. Baseline Configuration (Low Concurrency)

Configuration

Calculation

Result

2 Runtime Nodes, 1 Concurrent Process (default)

2 × 1

2 Consumers per iFlow

This configuration is highly efficient. In most landscapes, the 100 - queue limit is likely to be reached before the consumer limit.

2. High-Throughput Configuration

Configuration

Calculation

Result

2 Runtime Nodes, 10 Concurrent Processes

2 × 10

20 Consumers per iFlow

This single JMS iFlow consumes 20 of the available 500 consumers - 4% of the tenant's maximum consumer capacity. While this significantly improves throughput, it also reduces consumers available for other JMS-based integrations.

3. Large-Scale Deployment

Configuration

Calculation

Result

5 Runtime Nodes, 5 Concurrent Processes

5 × 5

25 Consumers per iFlow

If 20 integration flows are deployed with this identical configuration (20 × 25 = 500), the tenant reaches its maximum supported consumer limit. At that point, further JMS deployments or increased runtime demand may trigger resource allocation failures until capacity is freed or configuration is optimized.

The Retry Mechanism

When a message processing run encounters an unhandled exception, it is rolled back and flagged for redelivery based on the Exponential Backoff or Retry Interval configurations defined in the adapter. A few platform behaviors are critical to understand:

  • If an iFlow throws an unhandled exception, SAP retries that message indefinitely based on your configured Retry Interval and Exponential Backoff settings, there is no native "stop after N attempts" field.
  • To enforce a strict retry limit (e.g., stopping after 5 attempts), you must design an Exception Subprocess with a Router step evaluating: ${header.SAPJMSRetries} > '5'.
  • Setting the channel to Exclusive forces a single consumer thread to process messages strictly FIFO - a blocked retry pauses that entire queue.
  • Setting it to Non-Exclusive splits load across multiple concurrent processes, letting healthy messages bypass a single stuck retry, but ordering is no longer guaranteed.
  • Standard business or connectivity exceptions (e.g. HTTP 503) do not qualify for a native DLQ. A functional DLQ must be built by explicitly routing failed messages to a secondary custom JMS queue via an Exception Subprocess.

Production Guardrails Worth Building Into Every Design

To keep your integration flows with JMS queues stable, build integration patterns around these seven architectural realities.

1. Align Your Concurrent Processes to Target System Limits

A common pattern I have seen is teams increasing Concurrent Processes to clear a backlog faster, only to discover that the receiving system becomes the new bottleneck. Verify your backend can absorb that parallel load before ramping up concurrency, or you risk cascading database locks or HTTP 429 rate-limit errors that compound the original delay.

Example - Sales Orders into S/4HANA

[Online Store] → [JMS Queue: 10,000 orders backlogged] → [S/4HANA]

Scenario A — Concurrent Processes = 1:

  Single thread, ~2 orders/sec, clean but very slow (hours to clear).

Scenario B — Concurrent Processes raised to 50 to clear backlog faster:

  50 parallel threads hit S/4HANA at once.

  -> Database row locks on shared records (ERR_DB_LOCK_EXCEEDED)

  -> HTTP 429 Too Many Requests from the gateway

  -> All 50 messages roll back into the queue

  -> Exponential backoff pauses the whole queue

Result - Mass message rollback and exponential backoff clears the backlog SLOWER than with Scenario A.

2. Guard Against the Infinite Loop "Poison Pill"

SAP JMS queues have no built-in max retry limit for functional errors, by default they reprocess a failing message indefinitely. If a message contains a structurally broken payload and the queue is set to Exclusive Access for strict FIFO, that single "poison pill" sticks at the top of the queue and blocks every healthy transaction behind it until an engineer manually intervenes.

Example - Broken B2B Customer Onboarding

Requirement: strict FIFO (create must process before update).

Setting: Exclusive Access on the JMS Sender Adapter.

A partner sends a malformed/empty XML payload:

  1. Consumer iFlow picks up the malformed message

  2. XML Validator / Mapping step throws a parsing exception

  3. Unhandled exception -> rollback -> message returns to queue head

  4. Retry timer expires -> same broken message handed back

  5. Steps 1-4 repeat indefinitely - no platform-level 'stop after N'

3. Build a custom Dead Letter Queue

The standard JMS retry mechanism in SAP Integration Suite primarily handles message redelivery for processing failures. It does not automatically separate application-level failures, such as mapping errors or business validation failures, into a dedicated dead-letter queue.

The Role of ${header.SAPJMSRetries} - During redelivery, SAP Cloud Integration provides the native header SAPJMSRetries, which indicates the current retry count.

Catching the error inside an Exception Subprocess allows you to read this header using a Router step to check the number of retries ${header.SAPJMSRetries}. If ${header.SAPJMSRetries} exceeds your defined threshold, route the payload to a dedicated Error Queue and complete the Exception Subprocess normally. This prevents the failed message from being redelivered indefinitely and allows subsequent messages to continue processing.

4. Set Alerting Thresholds

SAP Integration Suite enforces tenant-level JMS storage limits. As queue storage consumption approaches the configured tenant capacity, message producers may eventually be unable to persist new messages when the limit is reached. The exact warning thresholds and monitoring behaviour depend on the tenant configuration and monitoring tools in use. As an operational best practice, teams should define proactive alerts below the maximum capacity threshold (for example, 60-70%) to allow sufficient time to investigate growing backlogs and prevent storage exhaustion.

5. Segregate Batch Volume from Real-Time Traffic

Queues compete for processing threads across your runtime nodes. Never combine a heavy, low-priority bulk load (e.g. a nightly 50,000-record inventory sync) with time-sensitive, low-latency events (e.g. real-time customer checkouts) in the same queue, the batch load will saturate available consumer threads and bury critical messages for hours. Isolate these into separate dedicated queues, e.g. Queue_Inventory_Batch and Queue_Orders_Realtime.

6. Monitor Queue Depth, Not Just "Completed" Message Logs

A common operational blind spot is relying solely on Message Processing Logs to assess end-to-end message processing. When a producer iFlow successfully writes a message to a JMS queue, its MPL is marked as Completed, indicating that the message has been successfully handed over to the JMS broker. This status does not confirm that the message has been consumed or processed by a downstream JMS sender iFlow.

If the consumer iFlow is undeployed, experiencing repeated processing failures, or unable to keep up with incoming traffic, messages remain in the queue until they are successfully processed or otherwise handled. As a result, queue depth and storage utilization can continue to grow even though the producer iFlow reports successful completion.

For this reason, operational monitoring should include JMS-specific metrics such as queue depth, message age, and storage utilization alongside MPL status. Monitoring these indicators provides a more complete view of the health of asynchronous integrations and helps identify backlogs before they impact tenant capacity.

7.  The Multiplier Effect - Runtime Node Scaling

When fine-tuning high-volume queues, treating the Number of Concurrent Processes in the JMS sender adapter as a global tenant limit is a misconception. SAP Cloud Integration runs on a distributed architecture where your configuration acts as a per-node setting. The true connection footprint hitting your backend follows a strict mathematical relationship:

Total parallel threads = Active RunTimeNodes × Concurrent processes per queue

If your iFlow uses 5 Concurrent Processes and the Cloud Integration tenant runs on 2 runtime nodes, each runtime node can create up to 5 JMS consumers for that iFlow. This results in a potential total of 10 concurrent message-processing consumers across the runtime nodes. Therefore, your backend could receive up to 10 parallel requests (depending on message flow and processing time).

Since runtime node scaling is managed by SAP, your primary job as a developer is defensive design. Keep your Concurrent Processes configuration conservative and consider the total consumer calculation so that increased runtime capacity does not unintentionally create excessive parallel load on backend systems.

At the end of the day, JMS on SAP Integration Suite is only as resilient as the architecture and configuration built around it.

Disclaimer: This post is based on standard SAP documentation and real-world project experience, individual tenant limits may vary depending on your specific BTP subscription tier. All architectural formulas and metrics are for guidance purposes and must be verified against your specific SAP Global Cockpit configuration before deployment.

entry.title: From SAP Integration Suite iFlow to MCP Tools: Exposing OData APIs to AI Agents
entry.content:
What is MCP and why does it matter for SAP?

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI agents discover and invoke tools at runtime. Instead of hardcoding API calls into your agent, you publish an MCP Server — a runtime endpoint that advertises a list of named, described tools. The agent reads those descriptions and decides autonomously which tool to call and with which parameters.

SAP Integration Suite now acts as an enterprise MCP Gateway: it hosts MCP Servers on the Integration Cell runtime, enforces OAuth2 authentication, rate limiting and audit logging, and lets you govern exactly which agents can call which tools.

In this post I'll show you how to go from zero to a working MCP Server in SAP Integration Suite (BTP Trial) — using the Northwind OData V4 API as the backend. Northwind uses the same OData V4 protocol as SAP S/4HANA and SuccessFactors, so everything here maps directly to real SAP backends.

The full end-to-end stack looks like this:

AI Agent (Claude Code)
   ↓  OAuth2 client_credentials  (scope: ESBMessaging.send)
MCP Server  —  Integration Cell runtime
   ↓  forwards Bearer token
Cloud Integration iFlow  (Northwind_API)
   ↓  CamelHttpPath + CamelHttpQuery passthrough
Northwind OData V4   https://services.odata.org/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/

Prerequisites

  • SAP BTP trial account
  • SAP Integration Suite activated (trial plan) with Cloud Integration + API Management capabilities
  • Integration Cell runtime enabled and Active (Settings → Runtimes)
  • SAP Process Integration Runtime service instance — plan integration-flow — with a Service Key

Step 1 — Enable the Integration Cell runtime

Go to SAP Integration Suite → Settings → Runtime Profiles and activate both Cloud Integration and Integration Cell.

Then go to Settings → Runtimes and confirm both show status Active. The MCP Server artifact requires the Integration Cell runtime — it cannot be deployed to Cloud Integration.

Step 2 — Create an Integration Package

Go to Design → Integrations and APIs → Create and create a new package. I named mine MCP_TEST_IS. All artifacts for this PoC live here: the iFlow and the MCP Server.

 

Step 3 — Build the iFlow

Inside the package, create a new Integration Flow called Northwind_API. The flow is deliberately simple: a single HTTPS Sender → HTTP Receiver with no transformation steps.

HTTPS Sender

Field Value

Address/northwind/*
CSRF ProtectedNo
AuthorizationUser Role: ESBMessaging.send

The wildcard address /northwind/* means a single iFlow handles all three entity paths (/northwind/Employees, /northwind/Customers, /northwind/Orders). SAP CPI automatically captures the dynamic segment in the Camel header CamelHttpPath, and the full OData query string in CamelHttpQuery.

HTTP Receiver

Field Value

Addresshttps://services.odata.org/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/${header.CamelHttpPath}?${header.CamelHttpQuery}
Query(leave empty)
MethodGET
AuthenticationNone

Important: Put the full URL including the query string directly in the Address field. Do not use the separate Query field — it escapes $ signs and breaks all OData parameters.

 

No Content Modifier. No Groovy script. The two Camel headers are populated automatically by the runtime.

Deploy the iFlow to the Cloud Integration runtime and wait for status Started.

Step 4 — Create the OpenAPI spec

The MCP Server needs an OpenAPI 3.0.1 spec. Each operationId becomes a tool name that the AI agent can discover and call. The description fields are what the agent reads to understand when and how to use each tool — write them for the agent, not for a human developer.

Two fields in the spec are specific to SAP Integration Suite:

  • info.x-targetEndpoint — the Cloud Integration runtime URL the MCP Server will forward calls to
  • servers[0].url — the Integration Cell base URL (used by clients to reach the MCP Server itself)
{
  "openapi": "3.0.1",
  "info": {
    "title": "NorthWind API",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "x-targetEndpoint": "https://<your-tenant>.it-cpitrial05-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/http"
  },
  "servers": [{ "url": "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind" }],
  "paths": {
    "/northwind/Employees": {
      "get": {
        "operationId": "getEmployees",
        "summary": "Get Employees",
        "description": "Returns employees. Supports OData: $filter (e.g. City eq 'London'), $top, $select, $orderby",
        "parameters": [
          { "name": "$filter", "in": "query", "schema": { "type": "string" } },
          { "name": "$top",    "in": "query", "schema": { "type": "integer" } },
          { "name": "$select", "in": "query", "schema": { "type": "string" } },
          { "name": "$orderby","in": "query", "schema": { "type": "string" } }
        ],
        "responses": { "200": { "description": "List of employees" } }
      }
    },
    "/northwind/Customers": { "...": "same pattern with getCustomers" },
    "/northwind/Orders":    { "...": "same pattern with getOrders + $expand" }
  }
}

Step 5 — Create the MCP Server artifact

Inside the same package, add a new artifact of type MCP Server.

Field Value

Namenorthwind_MCP
MCP Path/northwind-mcp
Source TypeHTTP Endpoint + OpenAPI Spec
URLhttps://<your-tenant>.it-cpitrial05-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/http

Upload the OpenAPI spec in the spec editor. The MCP Server parses it and shows 3 tools:

Tool name Description

getEmployeesGet Employees — OData filter, top, select, orderby
getCustomersGet Customers — OData filter, top, select
getOrdersGet Orders — OData filter, top, select, expand

Step 6 — Configure Policies (critical)

Under the MCP Server policies, configure:

  • Authentication: OAuth2, pointing to your XSUAA token URL
  • Authorization: change the scope from API.invoke to ESBMessaging.send

⚠️This is the most common mistake. The default Authorization policy scope is API.invoke. But the SAP Process Integration Runtime service key (plan integration-flow) generates tokens with scope ESBMessaging.send. If you leave the default, every call returns 403 Forbidden.

Step 7 — Deploy

Deploy the MCP Server to the Integration Cell runtime. Wait for status Started.

 

Step 8 — Test with curl

Get a Bearer token:

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
  "https://<your-tenant>.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=<CLIENT_ID>&client_secret=<CLIENT_SECRET>" \
  | python3 -c "import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")

List available tools:

curl -s -X POST "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind-mcp" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}'

Call a tool:

curl -s -X POST "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind-mcp" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/call",
    "params":{
      "name":"getEmployees",
      "arguments":{"$filter":"City eq '\''Seattle'\''"}
    }
  }'

Results: Nancy Davolio, Laura Callahan. ✅

Step 9 — Connect Claude Code as an AI agent

Register the MCP Server in Claude Code:

claude mcp add northwind \
  --transport http \
  --url "https://<your-tenant>.a.integration.cloud.sap/northwind-mcp" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN>"

Now ask in natural language. A few examples from the live demo:

"Show me employees in Spain"
Claude calls getEmployees with $filter=Country eq 'Spain' → empty result. It then self-corrects, calls again with $select=EmployeeID,FirstName,LastName,City,Country to list all employees, and responds: "There are no employees in Spain. The 9 employees are in USA and UK."

"Give me more details about Robert King"
Claude calls getEmployees with $filter=EmployeeID eq 7 and presents his full profile: Sales Representative, London, hired January 1994, reports to Steven Buchanan.

 

"Show me the orders Robert has handled"
Claude calls getOrders with the employee filter and summarises: 72 orders total, 3 pending, highest freight $830.75, countries served across USA, UK, Germany, France, Brazil, Austria, Mexico, Spain and more.

 

The agent chains tool calls autonomously. When Spain returned no results it self-corrected without being asked. This is the power of intent-shaped tools with rich descriptions: the AI decides how to query, not just what to call.

Key learnings

1. Scope: ESBMessaging.send — not API.invoke

The SAP Process Integration Runtime service key (plan integration-flow) generates tokens with scope ESBMessaging.send. The default Authorization policy uses API.invoke. Always change it or you'll get 403 on every call.

2. CamelHttpPath + CamelHttpQuery — no Groovy needed

A wildcard Sender address (/northwind/*) plus ${header.CamelHttpPath} and ${header.CamelHttpQuery} in the Receiver Address is enough to route all entities dynamically through a single iFlow. No Content Modifier, no Groovy script.

3. Query string goes in the Address field

The Receiver's separate Query field escapes $ signs and breaks OData parameters. Put the full URL including ?${header.CamelHttpQuery} in the Address field.

4. Intent-shaped tools beat endpoint-shaped tools

Name tools after business operations, not HTTP endpoints. Write descriptions for the AI agent — tell it when to use the tool and what parameters make sense. The agent uses those descriptions to decide autonomously what to call and how.

5. x-targetEndpoint is the routing key

The info.x-targetEndpoint field in the OpenAPI spec is what the MCP Server uses at runtime to forward calls to the iFlow. It must point to the Cloud Integration runtime URL, not the Integration Cell URL.

What's next

In the next post I'll cover Approach B — API Proxy: same result but the MCP Server sits in front of an API Artifact (deployed to Integration Cell) instead of an iFlow. That adds a full governance layer — rate limiting, IP blocking, payload protection — without changing a line of iFlow logic. The key new concept there is Trust Upstream MCP Authorization, which lets the API Artifact accept calls forwarded by the MCP Server without re-validating the token.
